title: #devex: 2487654321

source.messages:
Tara Lin: quick UX note — the new mini-preview row in the playground is promising but opening the schema still pushes the run history. during quick debugging that shift is distracting. can we avoid reflow?

Owen Gupta: Hmm, I ran into the same. Proposal: keep a fixed-height row for each fn with a 1-line summary (name + type badges + small chevron). expand should either layer an overlay anchored to that row or expand inline without changing page height.

Marta Ruiz: Overlay would preserve scroll position but may block copy/paste. Inline-but-no-reflow could work if the expansion overlays only the schema area (absolute positioned) and doesn't affect the DOM flow. that requires z-index management.

Ben Carter: agree with Marta. Also: button copy is inconsistent across the app — we have "Run", "Invoke", "Start". i vote for "Run" everywhere. small copy PR will be quick.

Tara Lin: a11y: screen reader announces "expanded" but not which function. need aria-label like "Schema preview for function SummarizeText (expanded)" and an aria-live polite update. and when entering edit mode focus should jump into the first input. preview expand shouldn't grab focus.

A11yCheckBot: Created issue #620: "playground mini-preview - aria-labels + focus rules". Checklist: [x] verbose aria-label, [ ] focus on primary input when editing, [ ] aria-live polite region for state change.

Owen Gupta: param chips in run history would help scanning: tiny icons + short label (TXT/NUM/FILE) at 14px, high contrast. they should be semantic with aria-labels like "title: text".

Marta Ruiz: visual guideline: chips use filled icon + 12px label, 16px line-height. hover shows full value. keyboard: chips should be reachable via tab when a run entry is focused.

Ben Carter: dev note: please standardize the run keybinding. add `R` shortcut in playground and show tooltip "Press R to run". i can add the tooltip copy if everyone's ok with `R`.

Tara Lin: poll: collapse previews by default (power users) or expanded by default (new users)? reply with :one: collapse, :two: expanded.

Owen Gupta: :one:

Marta Ruiz: :one: but detect first run -> expanded

Ben Carter: :one:

A11yCheckBot: Poll result recorded: 3 votes collapse, 0 expanded. Recommendation: default collapsed; auto-expand for first-time.

Owen Gupta: runtime caveat: collapsing result previews must not evict KV cache needed for replays. i'll open a runtime ticket to confirm cache TTL semantics.

Marta Ruiz: small copy tweak suggestion: change label from "Schema (auto-detect)" to "Schema preview — inferred" and add microcopy when detection fails: "Couldn't infer schema. Edit to continue."

Tara Lin: thanks — i'll draft the interaction spec: mini-row layout, overlay vs inline overlay rules, focus behavior, param chip ARIA patterns, and localStorage flag for preference. will link prototype.

Ben Carter: i'll take the copy PR once Tara posts the spec.

A11yCheckBot: Linked tickets and TODOs to project board: #620 (a11y), #621 (param chips), #622 (kv-runtime).

title: #devex: 1891234567

source.messages:
Maria Chen: Quick sync — shiproom tomorrow 10am PT for DevEx release. Need final signoffs on SDK 2.3.0, sample-kit v1.8, docs changelog cutoff by EOD today. Ping owners pls. :calendar:
Samir Patel: On SDK 2.3.0 — I have one open PR for a breaking-change note (docs + changelog) but tests are green. Can merge after a short review. @Ying can you sanity-check the example in quickstart?
Ying Zhao: I can check the quickstart this afternoon. Noted the new `StreamingClient` API; will validate snippets and update the code sample if needed.
Olga Kuznetsova: Sample-kit v1.8 has the last integration test failing on the sample that calls the embeddings route. I think it's a timeout default. Repro steps in repo: `samples/embeddings/test.sh` — I can push a patch to bump timeout to 12s but want to flag if that affects perf numbers.
Ben Carter: Olga, go ahead with the timeout bump and add a comment in the PR about why. We can run the perf job later. I'll own the perf regression check.
Joe Park: Docs changelog cutoff: I'll cut the CHANGELOG.md section for SDK and sample-kit at 5pm PT. Anything merged after that needs an explicit "post-cut" note. Everyone ok with that?
Samir Patel: Fine by me. I will not merge anything after 4:30 unless it's critical.
Ying Zhao: I'll finish review by 3:30 and open a tiny docs PR if snippets need edits.
Maria Chen: Copy. Owners: SDK -> Samir; Quickstarts/snippets -> Ying; Sample-kit -> Olga; Docs/changelog + release notes -> Joe; Release coordinator -> Maria.
Maria Chen: Quick checklist (putting here so it's easy to copy into shiproom agenda):

```
- SDK: merge all approved PRs, tag v2.3.0
- Sample-kit: fix flaky tests, tag release/v1.8
- Quickstarts: validate streaming example + node/python snippets
- CHANGELOG: cut at 17:00 PT (Joe)
- Release notes: draft + release highlights (Maria)
- QA: smoke on staging (Ben)
```

Maria Chen: If we hit any blockers, call for a quick sync 9am PT tomorrow.
release-bot: Reminder scheduled: DevEx shiproom at 2024-11-20T10:00:00-08:00 (tomorrow). Agenda link: https://go/release-rooms/devex
Olga Kuznetsova: Patch PR opened for sample timeout — https://github.com/redwood-inference/sample-kit/pull/482 — tests passing locally now, CI re-run in progress.
Ben Carter: I'll queue the perf job once Olga's CI passes. If perf reg >2% we'll rollback the timeout change and instead mock the external embedding call in tests.
Samir Patel: PR for SDK docs merged: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/1298 — includes a note about `StreamingClient` behavior.
Ying Zhao: Quickstart snippets updated and committed to docs branch, preview build passed. Small markdown fix left.
Joe Park: CHANGELOG cut pushed to main and tagged with "+release-notes" draft. Will paste notes into shiproom doc in 15m.
Maria Chen: Awesome — this is in good shape. Last ask: reply with a thumbs if you're blocking anything right now. :thumbsup:
Samir Patel: :thumbsup:
Ying Zhao: :thumbsup:
Olga Kuznetsova: :thumbsup:
Ben Carter: :thumbsup:
Joe Park: :thumbsup:
Maria Chen: Thanks team — see you tomorrow in shiproom.

source.text:
Jordan Blake: Quick thread  onboarding + SDK parity issue. New engineer did `redwood login` (writes ~/.redwood/token) then ran node sample and hit 401. Node SDK only checked REDWOOD_API_KEY. We should standardize precedence and improve diagnostics.

Priya Nair: Seen this pattern a few times. The inconsistency between SDKs is the root cause. We need: clear precedence, a diagnostic whoami, and a deterministic rotation flow.

Marcus Lee: My proposal (minimal, backward-compatible): precedence = 1) explicit client creds passed to constructor, 2) REDWOOD_API_KEY (env), 3) REDWOOD_SERVICE_TOKEN (env; service/project scope), 4) REDWOOD_TOKEN_FILE (env path or default ~/.redwood/token), 5) OS keychain/secret stores. Keep REDWOOD_ORG / REDWOOD_PROJECT only for routing.

Tasha Rivera: +1. Also add an opt-in client.metadata.auth_source string so samples and CI can assert what was used. Example: `client.metadata.auth_source === 'token_file'`. We must never log secrets, only the source enum.

Jordan Blake: For rotation: gateway should set `x-rw-auth-status: expired|revoked|invalid` on 401. SDKs: on expired -> try next source once, emit `auth:expired` event and surface a single actionable error with hint to `redwood login` or set REDWOOD_API_KEY; on revoked/invalid -> fail fast with `credentials-revoked` or `credentials-invalid`.

Priya Nair: Small UX win: sample apps print at startup -> "auth_source=token_file (from ~/.redwood/token)" or "auth_source=env (REDWOOD_API_KEY)". Also add `redwood whoami --json` that prints {org, project, auth_source}.

Marcus Lee: CI nuance: if CI env detected (CI=true or GITHUB_ACTIONS), default to prefer env and disable file fallback unless explicitly configured. Add option `preferEnvInCI` in CredentialProvider. Prevents accidental reliance on mounted token files.

Tasha Rivera: Implementation sketch for node SDK: a CredentialProvider chain with methods get(), sources(), on('fallback', cb). Example: `new CredentialProvider({order:['env','file','keychain'], preferEnvInCI:true})`. Expose `client.on('auth:expired', handler)` and `client.metadata.auth_source`. Unit tests for precedence + integration test mocking `x-rw-auth-status: expired`.

Jordan Blake: Security: telemetry on fallback should be opt-in and only include auth_source enum. No tokens, no org/project ids. Also mask anything that looks like a token in logs.

Priya Nair: Docs copy for quickstart: "If you used `redwood login`, the CLI token file will be picked up by SDKs (unless CI prefers env). To force env use `export REDWOOD_API_KEY=...`. Run `redwood whoami` to see the effective credentials." Add migration note in console after change.

Marcus Lee: I'll take node PR: CredentialProvider + refreshOn401 + startup logging for samples. Will include tests and a small whoami shim in the samples repo. Priya, can you draft the spec for python parity?

Priya Nair: Yep. Will draft and share link here.

auth-bot: Created ticket RDW-11022: "SDK credential precedence + whoami + auth-status header + CI env behavior" assigned to @Marcus Lee.

Tasha Rivera: Acceptance checklist: spec doc, node provider + tests, sample whoami + startup log, CLI whoami implementation, gateway header for auth-status. If we land all, onboarding tickets should drop.

Jordan Blake: Great. Please ping when PRs are open so support can validate in staging. Thanks!

source.text:
Sam Park: hey team — working on my first PR into main. can someone quick me on the expected flow, branch naming, and any style/commit rules? also what CI should I expect to see? :pray:

Kevin Li: welcome Sam! TL;DR: branch from main, keep PRs small, run local lint/tests, request 1 reviewer from your area. infra/runtime changes need extra reviewers. :wave:

Olivia Chen: quick checklist I send new folks: 1) branch name: feature/<JIRA>-short-desc or fix/<JIRA>-short-desc (e.g. feature/ABC-123-add-healthcheck). 2) commit message header: <scope>: <short summary> (50 chars max). 3) run pre-commit and ruff/black. 4) include a short PR description + test plan. 5) add labels (area/runtime/docs).

Olivia Chen: sample commit and branch:
```
git checkout -b feature/ABC-123-add-telemetry
git commit -m "telemetry: add request duration metric for inference path"
```

Ben Carter: CI you'll see: lint (ruff), unit tests (pytest), mypy for typed modules, and a smoke e2e on the dedicated pipeline for infra-affecting changes. If any check fails GitHub actions will post logs in the PR. 

Ben Carter: policy: small features => squash-and-merge. Larger refactors keep separate commits and @kevin for approval. If you touch serving/runtime, tag @eng-runtime or @eng-infra depending on scope.

Sam Park: thanks — how do I run the tests locally quickly? some of the CI jobs are long.

Kevin Li: for most changes run unit tests only:
```
# from repo root
make venv && source .venv/bin/activate
poetry run pytest tests/unit -q
```
For the full stack smoke (slower) use the docker compose helper:
```
docker-compose -f docker/dev.yml run --rm web pytest tests/smoke -q
```
Local lint/format: `pre-commit run --all-files` (should auto-fix formatting).

Olivia Chen: small tip: put integration or heavy tests behind markers so they don't run on every local dev run. See tests/README.md for markers. Also add a short `changes:` section in the PR description if user-visible.

onboard-bot: FYI — please review the PR checklist in #devex-docs before requesting reviews: https://redwood.example/docs/pr-checklist :robot_face:

Sam Park: perfect, thanks everyone — I'll push a branch and tag reviewers when ready. :thumbsup:

Kevin Li: ping @Ben Carter or me if CI is flaky. and welcome again!

Ben Carter: also drop a note in #new-hires with a link to your PR if you want extra eyes.

source.thread:
Jordan Kim: quick help — contractor following the Node quickstart hits 403 (billing_required) on the first SDK call. console shows "workspace created via SSO - billing pending". they accepted the SSO invite.

Lena Ortiz: can you paste the exact SDK snippet / curl (mask the token)?

Jordan Kim: snippet from sample app:```js
const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY });
const res = await client.generate({ model: 'gpt-4-redwood', input: 'hi' });
console.log(res);
```
Response: `{ "error": "billing_required", "message": "Workspace billing not active" }`

Priya Natarajan: two common things: (1) SSO-provisioned workspaces sometimes require an admin to accept billing terms even after the user accepts SSO, (2) local env/CLI can point at a different workspace id if the developer used an old token.

Jordan Kim: they used the repo sample .env and `npm start`. how do we quickly confirm which workspace the SDK is hitting?

Lena Ortiz: the SDK logs the workspace id if you enable DEBUG=redwood:client or run with REDWOOD_DEBUG=true. also `echo $REDWOOD_WORKSPACE_ID` if set. `redwood whoami` in the CLI will show the active workspace.

Jordan Kim: ran `redwood whoami` — shows workspace 8a7b (the SSO-created one) and status "billing_pending".

DevEx Bot: automated inspection — workspace 8a7b: created via SSO, no active payment method, policy 'org-only billing' enabled. Suggest: admin enable billing for that workspace or create an alternate personal sandbox workspace for quick testing.

Priya Natarajan: note for docs: SSO flows -> default to org billing to avoid orphan paid workspaces. that means contractors may need an admin to add a card or enable the trial. we should add a line in quickstart troubleshooting about "SSO-created workspaces may be gated".

Jordan Kim: admin is slow today. any temporary workaround for the contractor to continue deving?

Lena Ortiz: options: 1) Have the contractor create a personal test workspace from Profile -> Create workspace; that one can be used with the free onboarding credits. 2) Admin grants billing-owner role temporarily. 3) Use a local mock server for iteration (we have the redwood-local-dev docker image) if you just need fast loop without the hosted API.

Jordan Kim: contractor spun up personal workspace and set REDWOOD_WORKSPACE_ID to that id, re-ran sample and got first successful call. payload below:
```json
{"id":"resp_xyz","status":"ok","output":"hello"}
```

Jordan Kim: summary (for docs/FAQ): - SSO invite acceptance != billing enabled for the workspace - check `redwood whoami` / REDWOOD_WORKSPACE_ID / DEBUG logs to confirm which workspace is being used - quick workaround: personal workspace or mock server - error response could be enhanced with a Console -> Billing link and a short explanation

Priya Natarajan: filed DX ticket to add SSO section in quickstart troubleshooting and to append a Console link to billing_required payload.

DevEx Bot: Ticket created: DX-4310 "SSO workspace billing gating: quickstart guidance and error link"

Jordan Kim: thanks all, contractor unblocked. closing thread.

source.text:
Nina Gomez: Heads up — after updating to redwood-inference-js@2.2.0 our SPA bundles jumped ~30KB and a few edge routes started failing with "TypeError: client.retry is not a function" in prod only. Local dev (pnpm dev) is fine.

Ethan Park: that sounds like an ESM/CJS interop issue combined with tree-shaking. Did you change how you import the SDK?

Nina Gomez: we changed to `import * as RW from 'redwood-inference-js'` to avoid ts2322 warnings. Then we do `const client = new RW.default({ apiKey })`.

Theo Martins: `import * as pkg` + `.default` is brittle across bundlers — sometimes the default export is a wrapper. Also if retry is attached to the prototype in a separate file and that file was flagged as side-effect-only it can be removed by tree-shaking.

Nina Gomez: looking at node_modules/redwood-inference-js/dist, the retry logic lives in `dist/strategies/retry.cjs.js` and package.json has "sideEffects": false. The ESM bundle imports it via `require('./strategies/retry.cjs.js')` behind a conditional. Maybe the bundler dropped the require branch?

Priya Rao: edge runtimes often use esbuild-ish tree-shaking in Vite. If the conditional require is not statically analyzable, bundlers sometimes prune it. Also mixing CJS files inside an ESM package increases duplication (esbuild may include a small CJS runtime wrapper). That could explain the +30KB.

Ethan Park: quick repro steps: build the app with `pnpm build` and inspect `.next/server/chunks` (or Vite output) for duplicated retry code. Also run `npx source-map-explorer` or `vite build --analyze` to spot the added files.

Nina Gomez: ran analyzer: found `retry.cjs.js` present in node_modules and a bundled copy ended up in _another_ chunk as `vendor_retry.js` (duplicated wrapper code). The runtime error is because client instance's prototype doesn't include retry — looks like the SDK's ESM entrypoint conditionally assigns exports based on env and the bundler chose a path that omitted prototype patching.

Theo Martins: probable SDK fixes:
- avoid conditional require/require-from-esm patterns; use pure ESM entrypoints with static imports so bundlers see usage
- mark necessary files as side-effects in package.json (e.g. "sideEffects": ["./dist/strategies/retry.cjs.js"] ) if they need to run for initialization
- unify the single class/prototype implementation instead of patching via separate module side-effects
- export named helpers so apps can import `import { initRetries } from 'redwood-inference-js/strategies'` explicitly

Priya Rao: app workaround for now: explicitly import the retry module at top level: `import 'redwood-inference-js/dist/strategies/retry.cjs.js'`. That forces bundlers to keep it and avoids the prototype missing. Downside: extra bytes and fragile path coupling to dist layout.

Nina Gomez: we tried explicit import — fixes the runtime error and deduplicates (surprisingly the bundler then reuses the single copy). Bundle size drops back down ~12KB relative to the broken build.

Ethan Park: another app-side trick: pin to the previous SDK patch that had unified ESM entrypoint, or use `resolve.alias` to point imports to the CJS build to avoid interop paths. Not ideal long-term.

Theo Martins: SDK unit/integration tests should include a tiny consumer build with Vite and Next edge to catch these regressions. Also add a changelog note about bundlers and sideEffects.

Nina Gomez: opened internal issue RW-4477 and left reproduction steps + build artifacts: https://redwood.internal/issue/RW-4477

DevEx Bot: Created ticket RW-4477 for SDK: "Conditional require + sideEffects:false causes retry module to be tree-shaken -> runtime errors + bundle bloat" — https://redwood.internal/issue/RW-4477 :spiral_note_pad:

Priya Rao: tagging @eng-runtime and @devex for visibility. Any chance SDK could publish an alternate entry (like "exports.{ \"import\": \"./dist/esm/edge.mjs\" }") that keeps everything static?

Theo Martins: yes — exports map with separate entrypoints for `import`, `require`, and `edge` consumers is best. Also consider an opt-in `sideEffects: true` for a minor release to be safe while refactoring.

Nina Gomez: thanks all. We'll keep the explicit import in our app and wait for SDK patch. Leaving notes in onboarding doc about CJS/ESM pitfalls. :memo:

source.content:
Monica Patel: Post-beta check-in thread for SDK parity releases (stable). Please drop: (1) beta feedback we should address, (2) any remaining gaps from conformance, (3) go/no-go checklist status. Target is to cut stable tags this week.

Ibrahim Khan: Rolling up what we saw from beta cohort so far (6 customers + internal dogfood):
- 👍 fewer “random timeouts” reports, esp TS.
- still seeing some streaming disconnects behind enterprise proxies; the behavior is better (typed error + partial output) but docs need to be *very* explicit: “don’t auto-retry streams” unless idempotency key + app-level dedupe.
- one report: retry_count logging missing in Go when retries disabled (should be 0 not absent).

Ibrahim Khan: Go/no-go draft checklist (add/strike as needed):
1) Conformance suite green on main for py/ts/go
2) Retry standard defaults: 429 + 5xx + network; respects Retry-After (seconds + http-date)
3) Timeout model: overall + connect/read mapping documented
4) Streaming: consistent event semantics + disconnect typed errors; guidance on retries + idempotency
5) Error taxonomy: request_id always present where possible; retryable flag consistent
6) Docs PR merged (retries/timeouts + streaming guide) + migration notes published
7) Release notes + versioning aligned; deprecations called out
8) Observability/log fields: request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, streaming_disconnect_reason
9) SRE signoff (load impact) + rollback triggers in place

Dana Lo: TS status:
- conformance: green as of last night on release branch.
- remaining: I still have a TODO to ensure `AbortController` reason propagates into our `StreamingDisconnectError` consistently (right now it’s “client_abort” vs “canceled” depending on fetch impl). Not a behavior break, but impacts the `streaming_disconnect_reason` cardinality.
- beta feedback: 2 folks asked for “how do I disable retries completely?” snippet. I’ll add to docs + README.

Allison Grant: +1 on keeping disconnect_reason taxonomy tight. We should enumerate allowed values in docs (even if “best effort”). Suggested set:
- client_cancel
- read_timeout
- connect_timeout
- server_close
- proxy_disconnect
- unknown
(And please don’t leak raw error strings into reason; keep it stable.)

Mateusz Zielinski: Go status:
- conformance: currently red on one case: `Retry-After` http-date parsing with timezone. Our parser handles RFC1123 but chokes on a variant with GMT explicitly. Fix is small.
- logging: agree w/ Ibrahim, when retries disabled we should still emit retry_count=0.
- streaming: we’re not retrying streams; we return partial text + error with request_id if available.

Sahana Iyer: Confirming CI:
- Python: conformance green on main + release branch.
- TS: green.
- Go: failing `retry_after_http_date_variants` (Mateusz’s point). Everything else green.
We can hold the go/no-go gate as “all green across three” before tagging stable.

Sabrina James: Python beta feedback:
- biggest win: timeout knobs now actually work for streaming; that was the top complaint (SUP-29410).
- small doc nit: migration notes should call out that default overall timeout is now enforced (some users relied on “infinite” w/ streaming). We should suggest setting `timeout=None` / `timeout_ms=0` only if they have their own circuit breaker.
- also: our error messages are now redacted correctly, but one customer pasted logs that included `Authorization: Bearer ***` in debug. That’s acceptable, but we should make sure it’s always masked even in verbose mode.

Monica Patel: Re auth/logging: Marcus asked that we do NOT ever print raw tokens even in debug; only last-4 is ok. Sabrina, can you double-check python debug path? Dana/Mateusz too.

Nadia Rahman: Telemetry/log fields update: I reviewed samples from beta.
- Python: has request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms.
- TS: has request_id, retry_count; timeout_ms missing in one code path (streaming). Dana can you confirm?
- Go: has request_id + timeout_ms; retry_count missing when disabled (as mentioned).
We don’t need “phone-home” telemetry for this release, just consistent log/hook payloads.

Dana Lo: TS: yep timeout_ms missing in streaming hook payload. I can add it today; it’s available from config but not plumbed to the callback in stream path.

Dev Patel: Support POV from beta:
- We *need* the migration guide to include “what to send support” block: SDK version, request_id, retry_count, timeout settings, whether streaming, plus any proxy/corp network notes.
- In two cases, customers hit 429 storms and didn’t realize they had their own retry layer on top of ours. Can we add a docs callout “avoid double retries”?

Elliot Price: Docs status:
- retries/timeouts page is ready but waiting on final option names for TS/Go (timeout_ms vs timeoutMs etc) to avoid another round.
- streaming guide: has “do not auto-retry streaming by default” but can add the “double retries” warning.
Wei and I can merge within hours once SDK option names are final.

Wei Chen: I’ll add a boxed callout in all three language docs:
- “Avoid stacked retries (browser fetch retry, axios, custom wrappers, job runners). Prefer a single retry layer.”
Also adding “support bundle” snippet (what to provide) + example log line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE signoff conditions:
1) Default max attempts = agreed value (no surprises) and respects Retry-After.
2) Backoff jitter is in place (avoid herd).
3) Rollout has dashboards + rollback trigger defined (retry rate + 429 rate + error budget).
If Go still lacks Retry-After date parsing, that’s a no-go for stable tag IMO.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination proposal:
- We can tag Python + TS stable as soon as docs merge, but I’d *strongly* prefer a single announcement with all three.
- Option A: hold all until Go fix lands + conformance green.
- Option B: release py/ts stable now, keep Go as rc for 48h.
Given messaging complexity, I lean Option A.

Ibrahim Khan: Agree Option A. The whole point is “parity”; splitting releases undermines that.

Mateusz Zielinski: Ack. I’ll push Go fix in next ~2h + rerun conformance. It’s literally swapping to `http.ParseTime` with additional layouts and normalizing “GMT”.

ReleaseBot: ReleaseBot APP  [notification]
Go SDK: release branch build #1847 started (conformance + unit).

Monica Patel: Cool. Updated go/no-go checklist w/ owners:
- Go Retry-After http-date variants: Mateusz (BLOCKER)
- TS streaming timeout_ms in hook payload: Dana (BLOCKER for telemetry parity)
- Python token redaction in debug: Sabrina (SECURITY BLOCKER if not guaranteed)
- Docs: Elliot/Wei waiting on final option names (BLOCKER for announcement)
- SRE: Sean signoff after conformance green + docs merged

Sabrina James: I checked python: we mask Authorization header in our debug logger, but there’s one path where we dump “raw request headers” when `REDWOOD_DEBUG_HTTP=1`. That should redact too. I’ll patch + add a test that asserts no bearer token substrings make it to logs.

Dana Lo: TS patch up for timeoutMs -> hook payload in streaming path. Also standardized disconnect_reason mapping per Allison’s suggestion (client_cancel/read_timeout/server_close/unknown). PR in ~30.

ReleaseBot: ReleaseBot APP  [notification]
Go SDK: conformance FAILED (retry_after_http_date_variants). Log: "cannot parse Retry-After: Tue, 03 Feb 2026 12:01:19 GMT".

Mateusz Zielinski: Yep that’s the one I’m fixing. I didn’t include the “GMT” layout. Pushing a second commit.

Ibrahim Khan: While we wait: can we confirm final default values are what we agreed?
- max_attempts=3 (initial + 2 retries)
- base_backoff=200ms, max_backoff=5s, full jitter
- retries: 408, 409, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 + network errors (DNS, connect reset, TLS handshake, timeout)
- do NOT retry 400/401/403/404 (except 408)
- streaming: no automatic retry; surface partial output + typed disconnect

Allison Grant: That matches the standard we signed off on, with one nuance: 409 is retryable only for specific error codes (conflict due to idempotency key reuse vs generic). If SDKs can’t differentiate, safer to *not* retry 409 by default.

Ibrahim Khan: Good catch. Conformance currently expects: 409 retryable ONLY when error_code == "IDEMPOTENCY_CONFLICT_RETRYABLE" (or whatever the gateway emits). If missing, treat 409 non-retryable.

Monica Patel: Please ensure release notes call out that change explicitly; we got burned by TS retrying 400 in the past (SUP-29438).

Dev Patel: +1, support wants a one-liner we can paste: “SDKs retry only 429/5xx/timeout-ish, never 4xx except 408; 409 only with specific code.”

Wei Chen: I can incorporate that exact phrasing.

ReleaseBot: ReleaseBot APP  [notification]
Go SDK: release branch build #1848 started.

Mateusz Zielinski: Added layouts for RFC1123 w/ GMT + a unit test. Also ensuring Retry-After seconds wins if both headers present.

Sahana Iyer: Once Go build is green, I’ll rerun full matrix in CI and paste results here.

Elliot Price: For docs option naming: can we confirm final naming conventions?
- Python: `timeout`, `max_retries`, `retry_backoff`, `retry_jitter` etc
- TS: `timeoutMs`, `maxRetries`, `retry` options
- Go: `Timeout`, `MaxRetries`, etc
As long as docs show language-idiomatic names but same semantics + same defaults, we’re good.

Ibrahim Khan: Yep. Docs should emphasize semantics + defaults; language names can differ.

ReleaseBot: ReleaseBot APP  [notification]
Go SDK: conformance PASSED on release branch build #1848.

Sahana Iyer: ✅ Full conformance run summary:
- Python main: PASS
- TS main: PASS
- Go release: PASS
Waiting on TS + Python patches for logging redaction + timeout_ms field before we call this “go”.

Dana Lo: TS PR merged. timeoutMs now included in streaming hook payload; disconnect_reason values constrained.

Sabrina James: Python PR merged for debug redaction + test. Confirmed we never log raw bearer token (even in `REDWOOD_DEBUG_HTTP`).

Sean Gallagher: With conformance green + redaction fixed, SRE is ✅. Please make sure rollout is staged (announce, monitor retry rate + 429s, be ready to revert defaults behind env flag if needed).

Connor O'Brien: Cool. Next steps:
- Elliot/Wei merge docs + migration notes today.
- I’ll prep coordinated release notes + tags tomorrow 10am PT.
- Monica/Ibrahim: final go/no-go email/announcement in #announcements after tags.

Monica Patel: Go/no-go: ✅ GO pending docs merge. Thanks all. Drop any last-minute “known issues” we should list in release notes (esp streaming/proxy caveats).

Dev Patel: Known issue wording suggestion: “Some corporate proxies may terminate long-lived streaming connections; SDK will raise StreamingDisconnectError and may include partial output. Recommended: shorter `read_timeout`, app-level resume strategy, and avoid auto-retry without idempotency.”

Monica Patel: Perfect. I’ll include that as a “Streaming note” section.

source.messages:
Nikhil Sharma: Heads up: we need to update quickstarts/templates to match the new onboarding flow (signup -> API key -> first request -> first 100k tokens). Right now docs + examples are still sending people down the old “pick a model” path and we’re losing folks before first request.

Nikhil Sharma: Target outcome: reduce time-to-first-token. Email + console nudges are pointing to “Hello World” snippet and we should make sure all landing pages/quickstarts match that CTA.

Ben Carter: +1. From email v2 we’re linking to quickstart + “first request” snippet. If quickstart is mismatched, we’re basically A/B testing confusion :grimacing:

Marissa Cole: Also console nudges will be “Create API key” then “Run your first request”. If quickstarts start with “install SDK” but don’t show where to paste the key, we’ll keep seeing stalls.

Dylan Brooks: If you can share the exact copy/CTA you want in quickstart, we can make sure console nudge deep-links align (route + anchors). We’re trying to keep the step names consistent so funnel events aren’t messy.

Nikhil Sharma: Proposed quickstart order:
1) Create API key (link directly to console key page)
2) Set env var
3) Run minimal request (curl + TS + Python)
4) Optional: enable streaming
5) Next: templates/examples

Nikhil Sharma: I’ve started a docs PR to update onboarding docs + links: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/913
Also quickstarts PR with a minimal “first request” snippet: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-quickstarts/pull/412
And an examples template PR (minimal chat app): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-examples/pull/377

GitHub: PR opened: redwood-docs#913 "Update onboarding docs and email links" (author: @nikhil-sharma)
GitHub: PR opened: redwood-quickstarts#412 "Add hello-world activation snippet" (author: @nikhil-sharma)
GitHub: PR opened: redwood-examples#377 "Add minimal chat app template for activation" (author: @nikhil-sharma)

Kenji Nakamura: Quick copy question: are we emphasizing latency or cost in quickstart header? The nudge UI currently says “Get to first token in <2 min” and mentions “low latency” in the subtext.

Ben Carter: For lifecycle touchpoints we’re trying to lead with: fast first token + predictable cost. Reliability as a secondary. “Developer-first” tone.

Ben Carter: Maybe H1: “Make your first request” and a subhead: “Stream responses and see token-level usage in the Console.”

Paula Mendes: Please include instrumentation-friendly wording (don’t promise specific TTFT). We can say “in minutes” but not “<2 min” unless we’re ready to defend it.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also make sure links have UTMs if they’re used in email/retargeting. For docs/quickstarts, we can standardize:
`utm_source=lifecycle&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=quickstart`
and for console deep link:
`utm_source=console&utm_medium=nudge&utm_campaign=activation_checklist`

Nikhil Sharma: Noted. I’ll add UTM guidance in docs PR and keep headers conservative.

Nikhil Sharma: Minimal snippets (WIP) — feedback welcome:

Nikhil Sharma: ```bash
# 1) Set your key
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

# 2) Make your first request
curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-chat",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"Say hello in one sentence."}],
    "stream": true
  }'
```

Dylan Brooks: Looks good. One thing: can we ensure the model name matches whatever the email uses? If email says “redwood/verified-chat” and console default says something else, it’ll look broken.

Marissa Cole: Console nudge copy is currently model-agnostic (“Choose any model from the catalog”). But we do want the snippet to be copy/paste and succeed.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: if you hardcode a model, please pick one that’s stable and in all regions. We’ve had issues where region X doesn’t have model Y and activation funnels get noisy.

Nikhil Sharma: Good call. I’ll sync with Nadia/Mei on “safe default model” list, but for now can we use whatever the catalog marks as default for hosted? (or a “redwood/verified-*” alias)

Dylan Brooks: We can create/confirm an alias that resolves per-region (this is basically what routing does). But for docs, maybe just say “replace `MODEL_ID` with a model from the catalog” and include one known-good example.

Ben Carter: For activation, we want zero choice points. Folks drop when they have to decide. I’m in favor of one default that “just works” even if it’s not the fastest.

Tyler Benson: Also please make sure templates include the same event names or at least don’t block them. If examples use a different endpoint or an older SDK method, the activation event mapping might miss.

Nikhil Sharma: Re SDK: there’s a TS SDK PR improving missing API key errors (this helps onboarding too): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-sdk-typescript/pull/688
If/when that lands, we should update quickstart to show the nicer error message + link to key creation.

Kenji Nakamura: UX suggestion for docs page: add a small callout after “set env var” like “If you see `401 Unauthorized`, double-check your key and org.” In console we see a lot of confusion there.

Marissa Cole: + add a link to the new email preferences/unsubscribe page in console settings? (separate topic but we’ll likely get asked in docs comments)

Paula Mendes: That link is fine on settings/help pages, but I’d keep the activation quickstart focused. Don’t add extra nav until after first request.

Nikhil Sharma: Plan:
- Update docs PR 913 with step order + conservative copy + UTM guidance
- Update quickstarts PR 412 with snippets (curl/TS/Python), include optional streaming section
- Update examples PR 377 README to point to quickstart and keep template minimal
- Align model ID w/ a stable default (I’ll ask Logan/Marissa offline)

Dylan Brooks: If you want console deep links, the API key page route is stable. DM me the exact URL you want. Also please add anchors on the docs page so email CTA can jump to “Run your first request” section.

Ben Carter: Thanks. Timing: we’d love docs + quickstart updates merged before we ramp email volume (deliverability ramp plan starts end of week).

Nikhil Sharma: Got it. I’ll request reviews today. If anyone sees copy/flow issues, drop comments directly on the PRs.

source.text:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick thread on migration plan. We’re about to merge the standardized retry/timeout/auth + streaming semantics. Question: do we gate new defaults behind a feature flag (env var / client option) or ship as minor version defaults w/ deprecation warnings?

Monica Patel: +1. We need a call today because release train is getting tight. My instinct: avoid breaking changes, but also avoid “three different behaviors forever”.

Dana Lo: TS perspective: we can ship as minor if old behavior remains accessible + we don’t change signatures. But some changes *are* behavior-breaking (e.g., retrying fewer things, timeouts being enforced more aggressively).

Sabrina James: Python: same. Biggest risk is people relying on “infinite-ish” timeouts in streaming (aka hung connections that never error). The new timeouts will surface errors earlier. That’s good but could surprise.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: context cancellation semantics are also a change. Some customers currently pass context.Background() and rely on default client timeouts. If we set an overall timeout by default, they may see timeouts they didn’t previously.

Allison Grant: SRE hat: I strongly prefer we make the new retry defaults the default sooner rather than later. If we gate it behind a flag, most clients won’t opt in, and we’ll keep seeing retry storms / non-standards in the wild. But I’m also worried about surprise load if everyone flips at once.

Henry Cho: PM: customer expectations: “minor version shouldn’t break me”. If a customer upgrades and suddenly gets timeouts they never got before, Support will call it breaking, regardless of semver purity.

Ibrahim Khan: The changes we’re talking about:
- Auth env var precedence alignment + deprecations for old names
- Retry policy: only 408/409/429/5xx + network errors, honor Retry-After, exponential backoff w/ jitter, max attempts default
- Timeout model: overall + connect/read mapping per language
- Streaming: typed errors on disconnect, partial output semantics, no automatic replay unless user opts in

Monica Patel: Let’s separate “safe defaults” from “potentially breaking”. Can we ship deprecations in minor, and gate the potentially breaking runtime behavior behind an opt-in?

Dana Lo: For TS we can do something like:
- Default stays current in vX.Y
- Add `behavior: "standard" | "legacy"` (default legacy for one release)
- Print console warn once if legacy
- Flip default to standard in next minor or next major
But that still means two code paths.

Sabrina James: Python could do similar with `client = Redwood(timeout=..., retries=..., _behavior="standard")` or env var `REDWOOD_SDK_BEHAVIOR=standard`. But I really dislike leading underscore config.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go doesn’t love “behavior strings”, but could do `redwood.WithStandardDefaults()` option. Still effectively a feature flag.

Marcus Lin: Security: pls do not use env vars that encourage people to set secrets and behavior in the same place without clear names. Also don’t log env var values. If we add a behavior env var, keep it non-sensitive (obv) and make sure debug logs don’t dump the entire environment.

Allison Grant: Another angle: default retry behavior impacts platform load. If we keep legacy defaults for too long, we don’t get the client-side shaping we want (Retry-After respect + jitter). If we flip defaults immediately, customers may see fewer retries and think “Redwood is less reliable” because their app now sees 429/5xx sooner.

Henry Cho: Exactly. Also: TS had the bug where it retried 400 in some edge path (SUP-29438). Fixing that should be default ASAP, but it’s arguably a bug fix (ok for minor).

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed compromise:
1) Ship bug-fix changes as default in next minor (e.g., never retry 4xx other than 408/409/429).
2) For “timeout tightening” and “streaming disconnect typed errors”, ship as opt-in standard mode for one minor release.
3) Flip default to standard mode in following minor with loud release notes + migration guide.

Monica Patel: That seems reasonable. Timeline-wise we have ~8 weeks total, we’re midstream. If we do “opt-in for one minor”, we need to commit to flipping in the next.

Dana Lo: Concern: TS ecosystem expects minors to be safe. Flipping defaults in a minor later might still be seen as “why did it change”. But if we communicate deprecations + warn on legacy mode, it’s less surprising.

Wei Chen: Docs: if we do dual mode, docs need to show BOTH? Or just “standard mode” and add a callout “if upgrading and need legacy behavior temporarily, set X”. I’d prefer the latter to avoid docs bloat.

Sabrina James: +1: docs should primarily reflect the future state. We can add a short “Legacy behavior” section in migration notes.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, the legacy mode concept is weird because options are explicit. But we do have defaults. I can implement: default remains legacy for one release, add `WithStandardRetryPolicy()` and `WithStandardTimeouts()` options. Then in next release flip defaults.

Allison Grant: If we’re keeping legacy default for one release, can we at least add structured logging fields everywhere now? request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, behavior_mode. That helps us see who’s using what.

Ibrahim Khan: Yes. That’s in ENG-5129 anyway. We can make behavior_mode emitted always.

Marcus Lin: Please also ensure: no API keys in logs, and error messages don’t echo headers. If we add warnings, redact any token-like strings.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination: I need a concrete plan with version numbers. Something like:
- Python vA.B: add standard mode opt-in + legacy warnings, bug fixes always-on
- Python vA.(B+1): standard becomes default, legacy still accessible with explicit opt-out for 1 cycle
Same for TS/Go.

Monica Patel: Let’s decide on the mechanism naming so it’s consistent across languages. Not exact same API surface, but at least same concept and docs term.

Dana Lo: Suggest term: "standard" vs "legacy". (Avoid "v2" because people will think API v2.)

Sabrina James: Agree. Also avoid “strict” (sounds scary). “standard” is fine.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, we can call it “StandardDefaults” in option names.

Henry Cho: From customer comms: we should frame it as “improved reliability + predictability” and “more consistent timeouts”. Migration guide should have a “If you see new timeouts…” section.

Allison Grant: And a “If you’re getting 429s now” section with how to tune max attempts and respect Retry-After.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok, action items:
- I’ll update the migration plan doc to include 2-step rollout (opt-in standard for one minor, then flip default).
- Dana/Sabrina/Mateusz: implement `standard`/`legacy` mode switches in your SDKs (or equivalent) + warn when legacy.

title: #devex: 1768249973
preview:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick thread on migration plan. We’re about to merge the standardized retry/timeout/auth + streaming semantics. Question: do we gate new defaults behind a feature flag (env var / client option) or ship as minor version defaults w/ deprecation warnings?

Monica Patel: +1. We need a call today because release train is getting tight. My instinct: avoid breaking changes, but also avoid “three different behaviors forever”.

Dana Lo: TS perspective: we can ship as minor if old behavior remains accessible + we don’t change signatures. But some changes *are* behavior-breaking (e.g., retrying fewer things, timeouts being enforced more aggressively).

Sabrina James: Python: same. Biggest risk is people relying on “infinite-ish” timeouts in streaming (aka hung connections that never error). The new timeouts will surface errors earlier. That’s good but could surprise.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: context cancellation semantics are also a change. Some customers currently pass context.Background() and rely on default client timeouts. If we set an overall timeout by default, they may see timeouts they didn’t previously.

Allison Grant: SRE hat: I strongly prefer we make the new retry defaults the default sooner rather than later. If we gate it behind a flag, most clients won’t opt in, and we’ll keep seeing retry storms / non-standards in the wild. But I’m also worried about surprise load if everyone flips at once.

Henry Cho: PM: customer expectations: “minor version shouldn’t break me”. If a customer upgrades and suddenly gets timeouts they never got before, Support will call it breaking, regardless of semver purity.

Ibrahim Khan: The changes we’re talking about:
- Auth env var precedence alignment + deprecations for old names
- Retry policy: only 408/409/429/5xx + network errors, honor Retry-After, exponential backoff w/ jitter, max attempts default
- Timeout model: overall + connect/read mapping per language
- Streaming: typed errors on disconnect, partial output semantics, no automatic replay unless user opts in

Monica Patel: Let’s separate “safe defaults” from “potentially breaking”. Can we ship deprecations in minor, and gate the potentially breaking runtime behavior behind an opt-in?

Dana Lo: For TS we can do something like:
- Default stays current in vX.Y
- Add `behavior: "standard" | "legacy"` (default legacy for one release)
- Print console warn once if legacy
- Flip default to standard in next minor or next major
But that still means two code paths.

Sabrina James: Python could do similar with `client = Redwood(timeout=..., retries=..., _behavior="standard")` or env var `REDWOOD_SDK_BEHAVIOR=standard`. But I really dislike leading underscore config.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go doesn’t love “behavior strings”, but could do `redwood.WithStandardDefaults()` option. Still effectively a feature flag.

Marcus Lin: Security: pls do not use env vars that encourage people to set secrets and behavior in the same place without clear names. Also don’t log env var values. If we add a behavior env var, keep it non-sensitive (obv) and make sure debug logs don’t dump the entire environment.

Allison Grant: Another angle: default retry behavior impacts platform load. If we keep legacy defaults for too long, we don’t get the client-side shaping we want (Retry-After respect + jitter). If we flip defaults immediately, customers may see fewer retries and think “Redwood is less reliable” because their app now sees 429/5xx sooner.

Henry Cho: Exactly. Also: TS had the bug where it retried 400 in some edge path (SUP-29438). Fixing that should be default ASAP, but it’s arguably a bug fix (ok for minor).

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed compromise:
1) Ship bug-fix changes as default in next minor (e.g., never retry 4xx other than 408/409/429).
2) For “timeout tightening” and “streaming disconnect typed errors”, ship as opt-in standard mode for one minor release.
3) Flip default to standard mode in following minor with loud release notes + migration guide.

Monica Patel: That seems reasonable. Timeline-wise we have ~8 weeks total, we’re midstream. If we do “opt-in for one minor”, we need to commit to flipping in the next.

Dana Lo: Concern: TS ecosystem expects minors to be safe. Flipping defaults in a minor later might still be seen as “why did it change”. But if we communicate deprecations + warn on legacy mode, it’s less surprising.

Wei Chen: Docs: if we do dual mode, docs need to show BOTH? Or just “standard mode” and add a callout “if upgrading and need legacy behavior temporarily, set X”. I’d prefer the latter to avoid docs bloat.

Sabrina James: +1: docs should primarily reflect the future state. We can add a short “Legacy behavior” section in migration notes.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, the legacy mode concept is weird because options are explicit. But we do have defaults. I can implement: default remains legacy for one release, add `WithStandardRetryPolicy()` and `WithStandardTimeouts()` options. Then in next release flip defaults.

Allison Grant: If we’re keeping legacy default for one release, can we at least add structured logging fields everywhere now? request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, behavior_mode. That helps us see who’s using what.

Ibrahim Khan: Yes. That’s in ENG-5129 anyway. We can make behavior_mode emitted always.

Marcus Lin: Please also ensure: no API keys in logs, and error messages don’t echo headers. If we add warnings, redact any token-like strings.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination: I need a concrete plan with version numbers. Something like:
- Python vA.B: add standard mode opt-in + legacy warnings, bug fixes always-on
- Python vA.(B+1): standard becomes default, legacy still accessible with explicit opt-out for 1 cycle
Same for TS/Go.

Monica Patel: Let’s decide on the mechanism naming so it’s consistent across languages. Not exact same API surface, but at least same concept and docs term.

Dana Lo: Suggest term: "standard" vs "legacy". (Avoid "v2" because people will think API v2.)

Sabrina James: Agree. Also avoid “strict” (sounds scary). “standard” is fine.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, we can call it “StandardDefaults” in option names.

Henry Cho: From customer comms: we should frame it as “improved reliability + predictability” and “more consistent timeouts”. Migration guide should have a “If you see new timeouts…” section.

Allison Grant: And a “If you’re getting 429s now” section with how to tune max attempts and respect Retry-After.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok, action items:
- I’ll update the migration plan doc to include 2-step rollout (opt-in standard for one minor, then flip default).
- Dana/Sabrina/Mateusz: implement `standard`/`legacy` mode switches in your SDKs (or equivalent) + warn when legacy.
- Connor: propose version targets + release sequencing.
- Wei: docs should assume standard behavior; include migration callout for temporary legacy mode.
- Marcus: quick review of warning text + env var name if we add one.

Monica Patel: Also: let’s avoid env var if possible; prefer explicit client config. But we may still want env var for CI/testing to force standard mode for conformance runs.

Dana Lo: For TS conformance tests, env var is super convenient. But we can scope it to tests only? (not documented)

Marcus Lin: If you ship an env var in code, assume it will be discovered. That’s ok, just treat it as supported and document it. Don’t make “secret” switches.

Ibrahim Khan: Got it. We’ll document it as an alternative for fleet-wide config, but recommend code-based config.

Allison Grant: Please make sure legacy warnings are rate-limited (warn once). Don’t spam logs.

Sabrina James: Python: I can do a once-per-process warning. TS similar.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: we can log via injected logger; default no-op. We can add a single warning when constructing client.

Monica Patel: Decision captured: 2-phase rollout with standard mode opt-in first, then default flip, with clear migration notes. :thumbsup:

Ibrahim Khan: I’ll summarize in ENG-5120 and link this thread. Thanks all.

source.text:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a thread on the conformance test suite design. We need one set of fixtures + “golden” expectations so Python/TS/Go all prove the same behavior for auth precedence, retry/backoff, timeouts, streaming disconnects, error taxonomy.

Ibrahim Khan: Key question: where does it live?
1) Dedicated repo (e.g. redwood-sdk-conformance) consumed by each SDK
2) One “source of truth” directory in one SDK repo, copied/synced
3) Duplicated fixtures per repo (least ideal)

Sahana Iyer: +1 to dedicated repo. CI-wise it’s easier to version/pin (each SDK can pin to a tag). If fixtures live in python repo, TS/Go CI would need cross-repo fetch anyway.

Dana Lo: Dedicated repo sounds clean. My only worry is the harness code is language-specific. If the conformance repo includes runner code, it’ll either become a monorepo or we’ll have 3 harnesses.

Mateusz Zielinski: I’d keep the conformance repo *data only*: fixtures + golden files + a spec doc describing each test case + expected semantics. Then each SDK implements a runner that knows how to execute cases.

Sabrina James: +1 “data only”. We can do language runners in each SDK under `conformance/runner` that reads the shared fixtures. Otherwise we end up debugging Go CI by editing a central runner and it’ll get messy.

Allison Grant: From SRE perspective: make sure we include cases for 429 with Retry-After (both seconds + HTTP-date), 500/502/503/504, network reset, DNS fail, connect timeout vs read timeout vs overall timeout. Also include “do not retry” cases (400/401/403/404/422).

Ibrahim Khan: Agree on data-only repo. Then: how do we share it? Options:
- git submodule
- vendor as a tarball from GitHub release
- pull in via package manager (npm/pip/go modules) even though it’s data

Sahana Iyer: Please not submodules :grimacing:. For CI stability, I like “download a release artifact” (zip/tar) by version. Easy to cache, no git weirdness.

Dana Lo: For TS, npm “package that is just JSON files” is normal-ish, but we still need to support Go + Python. If we do GitHub release artifacts, all languages can curl/wget.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go tests can `go:embed` local files if present. But if we download artifacts at test time, that’s another moving piece. I can add a `make conformance-fixtures` that pulls the right version before running.

Sabrina James: Python can do similar: `python -m tools.fetch_conformance --version ...` and cache under `.cache/redwood-conformance/`.

Monica Patel: Stepping in: preference is simple + repeatable. Dedicated repo with tagged releases, each SDK pins a version. No “latest” in CI. Also we need a clear ownership model for adding/updating fixtures.

Ibrahim Khan: OK, dedicated repo (data only) + tagged releases + pinned versions in each SDK.

Ibrahim Khan: Next: what’s in fixtures vs golden files?
Proposal:
- `cases/*.json` for each scenario (name, description, request template, server script to simulate responses)
- `golden/*.json` for expected error shape / retry decisions / timing windows
- streaming cases: maybe `stream/*.txt` with SSE frames and an injected disconnect

Wei Chen: Docs angle: it’d be nice if each case has a short human-readable description we can reuse in docs/migration notes (“SDKs must treat Retry-After HTTP-date as …”). If cases are JSON, include `doc` field.

Dana Lo: Golden files: we need to be careful with anything time-based. Snapshot tests are brittle. For retries, maybe assert *sequence* (attempt count, retryable flags, backoff range) rather than exact sleep.

Allison Grant: +1. Don’t assert exact milliseconds. Assert:
- attempt_count <= max_attempts
- backoff monotonic-ish
- jitter within bounds
- Retry-After respected within tolerance (e.g., +/- 250ms)
Also log fields should be asserted (request_id present, retry_count increments).

Sahana Iyer: Also: normalize dynamic fields in golden output. e.g. strip `date`, `user-agent`, random boundary strings. In TS we often do a “stable JSON” transform before compare.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, I’d rather not do a huge normalization layer. Could the golden files specify which fields are required/optional and which should match regex?

Ibrahim Khan: Yes. Golden format could be “expectations”:
- required fields (exact match)
- optional fields
- regex fields
- numeric range fields
No nested JSON in the *slack thread* but in our actual fixtures repo we can do nested JSON obviously.

Sabrina James: Streaming tests: do we want to emulate the gateway? If yes, we need a tiny test server that can:
- send SSE chunks
- close connection mid-chunk
- delay between chunks
- return an initial 200 then disconnect
and we assert SDK surfaces partial output + typed error.

Dana Lo: In TS, fetch streaming is the pain point. We should include a case that disconnects after headers but before first chunk, and one mid-token.

Allison Grant: And include proxy-ish behavior: chunk buffering / long pauses. That’s how enterprise networks trigger read timeouts.

Ibrahim Khan: Implementation approach:
- Conformance repo contains “scripts” in a tiny DSL rather than code. Example:
  - `respond 429 retry-after: 2`
  - `respond 500`
  - `then respond 200 body: ...`
  - `stream chunk: "data: ..." delay_ms: 50`
  - `disconnect`
So each language can implement the same server behavior.

Sahana Iyer: That’s good. We can also provide a reference node server to execute the DSL, but then Go/Python tests depend on node in CI.

Mateusz Zielinski: +1 avoid node dependency in Go CI. I can implement a tiny HTTP server in Go to read the DSL, but then we’d be duplicating “server engine” in each SDK.

Sabrina James: Alternative: keep fixtures as recorded HTTP interactions and use a mock server library (wiremock-ish). But then streaming is hard.

Dana Lo: I think we bite the bullet and implement the DSL executor in each language. It’s not huge: parse JSON, run deterministic handlers, SSE writer.

Monica Patel: Directionally: yes. Keep it minimal and test-focused. Also: we need a place to track fixture changes and ensure they don’t break old SDKs unexpectedly.

Ibrahim Khan: Versioning rule suggestion:
- conformance repo releases: `v1.x.y`
- SDKs pin `v1.x.y`
- Breaking conformance changes => bump major (v2)
- Adding new cases is minor bump; SDKs can opt in by upgrading pinned version

Logan Wright: For telemetry/logging fields: add a conformance case that asserts the SDK emits a structured hook payload (or logger fields) with:
- request_id (if present)
- attempt_number
- max_attempts
- timeout_ms
- streaming_disconnect_reason
We can make it optional when telemetry is disabled, but hooks shape should be stable.

Sabrina James: +1. In python we’re adding `on_response`/`on_retry` hooks; conformance can check those callbacks get invoked with expected params.

Dana Lo: TS can expose an `onRequest` / `onResponse` callback too. For Go we can do functional options with hooks.

Ibrahim Khan: Action items:
- I’ll create a draft structure for the conformance repo + one or two sample cases (429 Retry-After + 500 then success).
- Sahana: can you propose CI wiring (download fixtures by version, cache)?
- Language owners: think about runner interface + hook assertions.

Sahana Iyer: yep, will do. We can add `CONFORMANCE_FIXTURES_VERSION` in each SDK CI and a small fetch step.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works. Please include a streaming case early so we don’t build the whole harness and then realize it can’t handle SSE edge cases.

Dana Lo: :thumbsup: also include a “do not retry on 400” case; we had a support ticket about that.

Allison Grant: +1 and a “respect Retry-After date format” case.

Monica Patel: Please document in the conformance repo README: who approves fixture changes (Ibrahim + language owners + SRE for retry policy related cases). Also, security will want to review any auth-related fixture content (don’t put real-looking keys in there).

Ibrahim Khan: Good call. We’ll use placeholders like `RW_TEST_KEY` and assert redaction behavior in logs.

Wei Chen: When you have the initial repo skeleton, tag me and I’ll ensure the case descriptions align with docs terminology (Retry-After, idempotency, request_id).

Ibrahim Khan: Will post the repo link in a follow-up thread once the initial PR is up.

and we assert SDK surfaces partial output + typed error.

Dana Lo: In TS, fetch streaming is the pain point. We should include a case that disconnects after headers but before first chunk, and one mid-token.

Allison Grant: And include proxy-ish behavior: chunk buffering / long pauses. That’s how enterprise networks trigger read timeouts.

Ibrahim Khan: Implementation approach:
- Conformance repo contains “scripts” in a tiny DSL rather than code. Example:
  - `respond 429 retry-after: 2`
  - `respond 500`
  - `then respond 200 body: ...`
  - `stream chunk: "data: ..." delay_ms: 50`
  - `disconnect`
So each language can implement the same server behavior.

Sahana Iyer: That’s good. We can also provide a reference node server to execute the DSL, but then Go/Python tests depend on node in CI.

Mateusz Zielinski: +1 avoid node dependency in Go CI. I can implement a tiny HTTP server in Go to read the DSL, but then we’d be duplicating “server engine” in each SDK.

Sabrina James: Alternative: keep fixtures as recorded HTTP interactions and use a mock server library (wiremock-ish). But then streaming is hard.

Dana Lo: I think we bite the bullet and implement the DSL executor in each language. It’s not huge: parse JSON, run deterministic handlers, SSE writer.

Monica Patel: Directionally: yes. Keep it minimal and test-focused. Also: we need a place to track fixture changes and ensure they don’t break old SDKs unexpectedly.

Ibrahim Khan: Versioning rule suggestion:
- conformance repo releases: `v1.x.y`
- SDKs pin `v1.x.y`
- Breaking conformance changes => bump major (v2)
- Adding new cases is minor bump; SDKs can opt in by upgrading pinned version

Logan Wright: For telemetry/logging fields: add a conformance case that asserts the SDK emits a structured hook payload (or logger fields) with:
- request_id (if present)
- attempt_number
- max_attempts
- timeout_ms
- streaming_disconnect_reason
We can make it optional when telemetry is disabled, but hooks shape should be stable.

Sabrina James: +1. In python we’re adding `on_response`/`on_retry` hooks; conformance can check those callbacks get invoked with expected params.

Dana Lo: TS can expose an `onRequest` / `onResponse` callback too. For Go we can do functional options with hooks.

Ibrahim Khan: Action items:
- I’ll create a draft structure for the conformance repo + one or two sample cases (429 Retry-After + 500 then success).
- Sahana: can you propose CI wiring (download fixtures by version, cache)?
- Language owners: think about runner interface + hook assertions.

Sahana Iyer: yep, will do. We can add `CONFORMANCE_FIXTURES_VERSION` in each SDK CI and a small fetch step.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works. Please include a streaming case early so we don’t build the whole harness and then realize it can’t handle SSE edge cases.

Dana Lo: :thumbsup: also include a “do not retry on 400” case; we had a support ticket about that.

Allison Grant: +1 and a “respect Retry-After date format” case.

Monica Patel: Please document in the conformance repo README: who approves fixture changes (Ibrahim + language owners + SRE for retry policy related cases). Also, security will want to review any auth-related fixture content (don’t put real-looking keys in there).

Ibrahim Khan: Good call. We’ll use placeholders like `RW_TEST_KEY` and assert redaction behavior in logs.

Wei Chen: When you have the initial repo skeleton, tag me and I’ll ensure the case descriptions align with docs terminology (Retry-After, idempotency, request_id).

Ibrahim Khan: Will post the repo link in a follow-up thread once the initial PR is up.
text:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a thread on the conformance test suite design. We need one set of fixtures + “golden” expectations so Python/TS/Go all prove the same behavior for auth precedence, retry/backoff, timeouts, streaming disconnects, error taxonomy.

Ibrahim Khan: Key question: where does it live?
1) Dedicated repo (e.g. redwood-sdk-conformance) consumed by each SDK
2) One “source of truth” directory in one SDK repo, copied/synced
3) Duplicated fixtures per repo (least ideal)

Sahana Iyer: +1 to dedicated repo. CI-wise it’s easier to version/pin (each SDK can pin to a tag). If fixtures live in python repo, TS/Go CI would need cross-repo fetch anyway.

Dana Lo: Dedicated repo sounds clean. My only worry is the harness code is language-specific. If the conformance repo includes runner code, it’ll either become a monorepo or we’ll have 3 harnesses.

Mateusz Zielinski: I’d keep the conformance repo *data only*: fixtures + golden files + a spec doc describing each test case + expected semantics. Then each SDK implements a runner that knows how to execute cases.

Sabrina James: +1 “data only”. We can do language runners in each SDK under `conformance/runner` that reads the shared fixtures. Otherwise we end up debugging Go CI by editing a central runner and it’ll get messy.

Allison Grant: From SRE perspective: make sure we include cases for 429 with Retry-After (both seconds + HTTP-date), 500/502/503/504, network reset, DNS fail, connect timeout vs read timeout vs overall timeout. Also include “do not retry” cases (400/401/403/404/422).

Ibrahim Khan: Agree on data-only repo. Then: how do we share it? Options:
- git submodule
- vendor as a tarball from GitHub release
- pull in via package manager (npm/pip/go modules) even though it’s data

Sahana Iyer: Please not submodules :grimacing:. For CI stability, I like “download a release artifact” (zip/tar) by version. Easy to cache, no git weirdness.

Dana Lo: For TS, npm “package that is just JSON files” is normal-ish, but we still need to support Go + Python. If we do GitHub release artifacts, all languages can curl/wget.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go tests can `go:embed` local files if present. But if we download artifacts at test time, that’s another moving piece. I can add a `make conformance-fixtures` that pulls the right version before running.

Sabrina James: Python can do similar: `python -m tools.fetch_conformance --version ...` and cache under `.cache/redwood-conformance/`.

Monica Patel: Stepping in: preference is simple + repeatable. Dedicated repo with tagged releases, each SDK pins a version. No “latest” in CI. Also we need a clear ownership model for adding/updating fixtures.

Ibrahim Khan: OK, dedicated repo (data only) + tagged releases + pinned versions in each SDK.

Ibrahim Khan: Next: what’s in fixtures vs golden files?
Proposal:
- `cases/*.json` for each scenario (name, description, request template, server script to simulate responses)
- `golden/*.json` for expected error shape / retry decisions / timing windows
- streaming cases: maybe `stream/*.txt` with SSE frames and an injected disconnect

Wei Chen: Docs angle: it’d be nice if each case has a short human-readable description we can reuse in docs/migration notes (“SDKs must treat Retry-After HTTP-date as …”). If cases are JSON, include `doc` field.

Dana Lo: Golden files: we need to be careful with anything time-based. Snapshot tests are brittle. For retries, maybe assert *sequence* (attempt count, retryable flags, backoff range) rather than exact sleep.

Allison Grant: +1. Don’t assert exact milliseconds. Assert:
- attempt_count <= max_attempts
- backoff monotonic-ish
- jitter within bounds
- Retry-After respected within tolerance (e.g., +/- 250ms)
Also log fields should be asserted (request_id present, retry_count increments).

Sahana Iyer: Also: normalize dynamic fields in golden output. e.g. strip `date`, `user-agent`, random boundary strings. In TS we often do a “stable JSON” transform before compare.

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, I’d rather not do a huge normalization layer. Could the golden files specify which fields are required/optional and which should match regex?

Ibrahim Khan: Yes. Golden format could be “expectations”:
- required fields (exact match)
- optional fields
- regex fields
- numeric range fields
No nested JSON in the *slack thread* but in our actual fixtures repo we can do nested JSON obviously.

Sabrina James: Streaming tests: do we want to emulate the gateway? If yes, we need a tiny test server that can:
- send SSE chunks
- close connection mid-chunk
- delay between chunks
- return an initial 200 then disconnect
and we assert SDK surfaces partial output + typed error.

Dana Lo: In TS, fetch streaming is the pain point. We should include a case that disconnects after headers but before first chunk, and one mid-token.

Allison Grant: And include proxy-ish behavior: chunk buffering / long pauses. That’s how enterprise networks trigger read timeouts.

  - `respond 429 retry-after: 2`
  - `respond 500`
  - `then respond 200 body: ...`

  - `disconnect`
So each language can implement the same server behavior.

Sabrina James: Alternative: keep fixtures as recorded HTTP interactions and use a mock server library (wiremock-ish). But then streaming is hard.

Monica Patel: Directionally: yes. Keep it minimal and test-focused. Also: we need a place to track fixture changes and ensure they don’t break old SDKs unexpectedly.

- Adding new cases is minor bump; SDKs can opt in by upgrading pinned version

- timeout_ms

Dana Lo: TS can expose an `onRequest` / `onResponse` callback too. For Go we can do functional options with hooks.

- Sahana: can you propose CI wiring (download fixtures by version, cache)?
- Language owners: think about runner interface + hook assertions.
