source.text:
Ibrahim Khan: Seeing a cluster of reports about streaming disconnects behind corp proxies (Zscaler/Bluecoat-ish). Anyone else seeing this? Need to decide standard SDK behavior for “server went away mid-stream” vs “proxy buffered then resets”.

Owen Phillips: yup, SUP-29505 is basically that. Customer on enterprise network, stream works on home wifi, breaks in office. They get partial tokens then the iterator just ends (no error) in Python. They assumed completion was “done”. :facepalm:

Sabrina James: Python currently treats EOF on the http stream as StopIteration w/o raising unless the parser hits invalid JSON first. We need to distinguish “graceful done” vs “socket closed mid-event”.

Dana Lo: TS side similar-ish. fetch stream reader returns `{ done: true }` and we stop; we only throw if we can’t parse an event. If proxy terminates cleanly, we think it’s done.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SSE decoder reads until io.EOF, returns nil. We do not validate we got a `finish_reason` / `[DONE]` marker. So we can silently truncate.

Allison Grant: From SRE POV, silent truncation is worst case. Prefer we surface a typed error that includes request_id + how many events we saw. But careful: some proxies may strip trailing bytes; we don’t want infinite retries.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Proposed standard: streaming must be “explicitly finished” by server event (e.g. `event: done` or `data: [DONE]` or a final message with `finish_reason`). If the connection closes before that, raise `StreamingDisconnectedError` w/ `request_id`, `received_events`, and `partial_output` (if available).

Owen Phillips: Here’s the customer log snippet they sent (sanitized). Note they see `200` then ~10 seconds of tokens then boom.
```text
> POST /v1/chat/completions HTTP/2
> Host: api.redwoodinference.com
> Accept: text/event-stream
> Authorization: Bearer rwk_live_***
>
< HTTP/2 200
< content-type: text/event-stream
< x-request-id: req_01HR8J8J3M5J7J9W...
< 
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":"Hello"}
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":" there"}
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":"!"}
< (… ~20 more …)
< 
* transfer closed with outstanding read data remaining
* TLSv1.3 (IN), TLS alert, close notify (256):
* Connection #0 to host api.redwoodinference.com left intact
curl: (18) transfer closed with outstanding read data remaining
```

Sabrina James: That curl error (“outstanding read data remaining”) usually means the stream ended unexpectedly while curl expected more bytes (chunked encoding mismatch / proxy). In httpx we see something like `RemoteProtocolError: peer closed connection without sending complete message body`.

Dana Lo: In node 18+ with undici/fetch, we saw `TypeError: terminated` sometimes. In browsers it can be `NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource.`

Mateusz Zielinski: Go behind certain proxies: `unexpected EOF` from bufio reader, or just `EOF`.

Ibrahim Khan: Can we capture representative errors per language for conformance fixtures? Like “mid-chunk FIN” vs “half-closed then read timeout”?

Allison Grant: Yes, but minimal set. At least:
1) connection drops before DONE marker
2) proxy buffers, then releases large chunk, then drops
3) server sends DONE but client doesn’t read it due to cancellation (should be treated as user cancel, not error)

Sabrina James: For Python: we can track whether we ever saw “done” marker. Our SSE parser already detects `data: [DONE]` in some paths. We just don’t enforce it.

Dana Lo: TS: we can enforce same in the streaming iterator. If `done=true` and we never saw DONE marker, throw.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: same. We can keep a boolean `sawTerminalEvent` and if loop exits due to io.EOF and false -> error.

Ethan Park: Quick note from platform: gateway guarantees `x-request-id` header on initial response. For mid-stream errors, we can’t always send structured JSON obviously, but request_id should still be available from headers. If SDKs always plumb it through, support can correlate.

Owen Phillips: Support request: please make sure the exception text includes “stream disconnected” and the request_id. Customers paste errors into tickets, we grep.

Ibrahim Khan: Concrete proposal for error shape across SDKs:
- name: `StreamingDisconnectedError`
- fields: `message`, `request_id`, `status_code` (if known; usually 200), `received_event_count`, `received_bytes` (optional), `partial_output` (best effort), `retryable=false` by default
- plus `cause`/`inner` error if available

Allison Grant: re retryable: default false, but maybe `retryable=true` if disconnect happens before first token? That’s likely safe-ish.

Dana Lo: careful: even before first token, server may have started compute. But if it disconnects before any deltas, the customer sees nothing. They might want a retry. We can document: “Retry safe only with idempotency key or when side effects are absent.”

Sabrina James: Agree. The SDK shouldn’t auto-retry a streaming request. But we can expose a helper / doc recipe:
- set idempotency key header
- on `StreamingDisconnectedError`, retry once with same idempotency key

Ibrahim Khan: Another nuance: some customers treat streaming as UI typing effect. Retrying mid-stream can duplicate tokens in UI. We should ensure error includes partial output so they can decide.

Mateusz Zielinski: Implementation note Go: we can accumulate partial text from deltas as we parse. If error triggers, return the partial text on the error.

Dana Lo: TS: same, we can accumulate a `StringBuilder` (or array join) and attach `partial`.

Sabrina James: Python: we can attach `partial_text` to exception. But for chat outputs, might be multiple choices; we can keep partial per choice index.

Ibrahim Khan: Let’s keep it simple for v1: `partial_output` as a JSON-ish string in the error message? But we shouldn’t do nested JSON in the error shape standard. Hmm.

Allison Grant: We can define `partial_output` as “string”, and if multiple choices, join them with separators + mention in docs. The important thing is not losing *something*.

Monica Patel: +1 to typed error and “silent truncation = bug”. Also please make sure we don’t log tokens by default. Partial output should only be on the exception object returned to caller, not in SDK logs.

Sabrina James: Logging: we should log request_id, stream_disconnect_reason, retry_count (0), and whether DONE was seen. But not token content.

Dana Lo: Here’s what I think the TS code path would look like (pseudo):
```ts
let sawDone = false;
let partial = "";
for await (const event of streamSSE(res.body)) {
  if (event.type === "done" || event.data === "[DONE]") { sawDone = true; break; }
  if (event.type === "delta") partial += event.delta;
  yield event;
}
if (!sawDone && !userAborted) {
  throw new StreamingDisconnectedError({ requestId, partialOutput: partial, receivedEventCount });
}
```

Mateusz Zielinski: Go analog:
```go
sawDone := false
partial := strings.Builder{}
for {
  ev, err := dec.Next()
  if err != nil {
    if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) && !sawDone && !ctx.Err() {
      return &StreamingDisconnectedError{RequestID: rid, PartialOutput: partial.String()}
    }
    return err
  }
  if ev.Done { sawDone = true; return nil }
  if ev.Delta != "" { partial.WriteString(ev.Delta) }
  ch <- ev
}
```

Sabrina James: Python sketch:
```py
saw_done = False
partial = []
try:
  for event in _iter_sse(resp):
    if event.is_done:
      saw_done = True
      break
    if event.type == "delta":
      partial.append(event.delta)
    yield event
except Exception as e:
  raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial), cause=e)
finally:
  if not saw_done and not user_cancelled:
    raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial))
```
Need to be careful not to double-raise (except/finally). But directionally.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: we should document a “proxy mode” recommendation: set longer read timeout for streaming and disable any default response decompression issues? Some proxies mess with gzip.

Allison Grant: For timeouts: streaming should use “overall = infinite-ish” but “read timeout” should be something like 60s idle. If no bytes for 60s, abort with `StreamIdleTimeoutError` (different from disconnect). That’s another common confusion.

Dana Lo: That’s good. In TS we can implement idle timer that resets on each chunk.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go can use `ReadDeadline` via custom transport / wrapper reader.

Owen Phillips: Customer asked: “Can you just auto-reconnect and continue?” We should have a crisp answer in docs: no resume support; safe retry requires idempotency key; otherwise you may get different outputs / duplicates.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update the parity matrix row for streaming disconnect semantics + propose acceptance criteria
- language owners: implement “DONE required” check + new error type
- Allison: confirm default idle timeout guidance w/ SRE
- Owen: update support macro to ask for request_id + SDK version + whether behind proxy

Ibrahim Khan: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a conformance fixture: server sends 200 + N delta events then closes without DONE. Expected: error type + partial output present + request_id propagated.

Dana Lo: One more edge: user cancellation (AbortController / context cancel) should not be treated as disconnect error. We should map that to `RequestCancelledError`.

Sabrina James: yes. For python, `asyncio.CancelledError` -> cancelled error; and ensure we don’t wrap it as StreamingDisconnected.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go ctx cancellation should return ctx.Err() (or typed wrapper) and not `StreamingDisconnectedError`.

Allison Grant: Please also ensure telemetry field `streaming_disconnect_reason` has controlled values: `eof_before_done`, `idle_timeout`, `user_cancelled`, `parse_error`, `network_error`.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. I’ll draft the standard wording and share in this thread.

Ibrahim Khan: Draft wording for standard (WIP):
- A streaming response is considered successful only if terminal marker is observed (`[DONE]` or equivalent).
- If the transport ends before terminal marker and caller did not cancel, SDK MUST raise `StreamingDisconnectedError` and include `request_id` and best-effort `partial_output`.
- SDK MUST NOT automatically retry streaming requests.
- SDK SHOULD provide guidance for manual retry using idempotency keys.
Feedback welcome.

Monica Patel: Looks right. Make sure it calls out proxies explicitly (common cause) so customers don’t assume it’s always Redwood server-side.

Owen Phillips: +1, and include a troubleshooting blurb: “Try from non-proxied network” / “Ask IT to bypass SSL inspection for api.redwoodinference.com” etc.

Dana Lo: ok from TS side.

Mateusz Zielinski: ok from Go side.

Sabrina James: ok from Python side. I’ll file an issue/PR to enforce DONE semantics + add the error.

Allison Grant: ok. Just ensure we respect privacy around partial output (never log).

```ts
  throw new StreamingDisconnectedError({ requestId, partialOutput: partial, receivedEventCount });
}
```

Mateusz Zielinski: Go analog:
```go
sawDone := false
partial := strings.Builder{}
for {
  ev, err := dec.Next()
  if err != nil {
    if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) && !sawDone && !ctx.Err() {
      return &StreamingDisconnectedError{RequestID: rid, PartialOutput: partial.String()}
    }
    return err
  }
  if ev.Done { sawDone = true; return nil }
  if ev.Delta != "" { partial.WriteString(ev.Delta) }
  ch <- ev
}
```

Sabrina James: Python sketch:
```py
saw_done = False
partial = []
try:
  for event in _iter_sse(resp):
    if event.is_done:
      saw_done = True
      break
    if event.type == "delta":
      partial.append(event.delta)
    yield event
except Exception as e:
  raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial), cause=e)
finally:
  if not saw_done and not user_cancelled:
    raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial))
```
Need to be careful not to double-raise (except/finally). But directionally.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: we should document a “proxy mode” recommendation: set longer read timeout for streaming and disable any default response decompression issues? Some proxies mess with gzip.

Allison Grant: For timeouts: streaming should use “overall = infinite-ish” but “read timeout” should be something like 60s idle. If no bytes for 60s, abort with `StreamIdleTimeoutError` (different from disconnect). That’s another common confusion.

Dana Lo: That’s good. In TS we can implement idle timer that resets on each chunk.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go can use `ReadDeadline` via custom transport / wrapper reader.

Owen Phillips: Customer asked: “Can you just auto-reconnect and continue?” We should have a crisp answer in docs: no resume support; safe retry requires idempotency key; otherwise you may get different outputs / duplicates.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update the parity matrix row for streaming disconnect semantics + propose acceptance criteria
- language owners: implement “DONE required” check + new error type
- Allison: confirm default idle timeout guidance w/ SRE
- Owen: update support macro to ask for request_id + SDK version + whether behind proxy

Ibrahim Khan: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a conformance fixture: server sends 200 + N delta events then closes without DONE. Expected: error type + partial output present + request_id propagated.

Dana Lo: One more edge: user cancellation (AbortController / context cancel) should not be treated as disconnect error. We should map that to `RequestCancelledError`.

Sabrina James: yes. For python, `asyncio.CancelledError` -> cancelled error; and ensure we don’t wrap it as StreamingDisconnected.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go ctx cancellation should return ctx.Err() (or typed wrapper) and not `StreamingDisconnectedError`.

Allison Grant: Please also ensure telemetry field `streaming_disconnect_reason` has controlled values: `eof_before_done`, `idle_timeout`, `user_cancelled`, `parse_error`, `network_error`.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. I’ll draft the standard wording and share in this thread.

Ibrahim Khan: Draft wording for standard (WIP):
- A streaming response is considered successful only if terminal marker is observed (`[DONE]` or equivalent).
- If the transport ends before terminal marker and caller did not cancel, SDK MUST raise `StreamingDisconnectedError` and include `request_id` and best-effort `partial_output`.
- SDK MUST NOT automatically retry streaming requests.
- SDK SHOULD provide guidance for manual retry using idempotency keys.
Feedback welcome.

Monica Patel: Looks right. Make sure it calls out proxies explicitly (common cause) so customers don’t assume it’s always Redwood server-side.

Owen Phillips: +1, and include a troubleshooting blurb: “Try from non-proxied network” / “Ask IT to bypass SSL inspection for api.redwoodinference.com” etc.

Dana Lo: ok from TS side.

Mateusz Zielinski: ok from Go side.

Sabrina James: ok from Python side. I’ll file an issue/PR to enforce DONE semantics + add the error.

Allison Grant: ok. Just ensure we respect privacy around partial output (never log).
text:
Ibrahim Khan: Seeing a cluster of reports about streaming disconnects behind corp proxies (Zscaler/Bluecoat-ish). Anyone else seeing this? Need to decide standard SDK behavior for “server went away mid-stream” vs “proxy buffered then resets”.

Owen Phillips: yup, SUP-29505 is basically that. Customer on enterprise network, stream works on home wifi, breaks in office. They get partial tokens then the iterator just ends (no error) in Python. They assumed completion was “done”. :facepalm:

Sabrina James: Python currently treats EOF on the http stream as StopIteration w/o raising unless the parser hits invalid JSON first. We need to distinguish “graceful done” vs “socket closed mid-event”.

Dana Lo: TS side similar-ish. fetch stream reader returns `{ done: true }` and we stop; we only throw if we can’t parse an event. If proxy terminates cleanly, we think it’s done.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SSE decoder reads until io.EOF, returns nil. We do not validate we got a `finish_reason` / `[DONE]` marker. So we can silently truncate.

Allison Grant: From SRE POV, silent truncation is worst case. Prefer we surface a typed error that includes request_id + how many events we saw. But careful: some proxies may strip trailing bytes; we don’t want infinite retries.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Proposed standard: streaming must be “explicitly finished” by server event (e.g. `event: done` or `data: [DONE]` or a final message with `finish_reason`). If the connection closes before that, raise `StreamingDisconnectedError` w/ `request_id`, `received_events`, and `partial_output` (if available).

Owen Phillips: Here’s the customer log snippet they sent (sanitized). Note they see `200` then ~10 seconds of tokens then boom.
```text
> POST /v1/chat/completions HTTP/2
> Host: api.redwoodinference.com
> Accept: text/event-stream
> Authorization: Bearer rwk_live_***
>
< HTTP/2 200
< content-type: text/event-stream
< x-request-id: req_01HR8J8J3M5J7J9W...
< 
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":"Hello"}
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":" there"}
< data: {"type":"delta","delta":"!"}
< (… ~20 more …)
< 
* transfer closed with outstanding read data remaining
* TLSv1.3 (IN), TLS alert, close notify (256):
* Connection #0 to host api.redwoodinference.com left intact
curl: (18) transfer closed with outstanding read data remaining
```

Sabrina James: That curl error (“outstanding read data remaining”) usually means the stream ended unexpectedly while curl expected more bytes (chunked encoding mismatch / proxy). In httpx we see something like `RemoteProtocolError: peer closed connection without sending complete message body`.

Dana Lo: In node 18+ with undici/fetch, we saw `TypeError: terminated` sometimes. In browsers it can be `NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource.`

Mateusz Zielinski: Go behind certain proxies: `unexpected EOF` from bufio reader, or just `EOF`.

Ibrahim Khan: Can we capture representative errors per language for conformance fixtures? Like “mid-chunk FIN” vs “half-closed then read timeout”?

Allison Grant: Yes, but minimal set. At least:
1) connection drops before DONE marker
2) proxy buffers, then releases large chunk, then drops
3) server sends DONE but client doesn’t read it due to cancellation (should be treated as user cancel, not error)

Sabrina James: For Python: we can track whether we ever saw “done” marker. Our SSE parser already detects `data: [DONE]` in some paths. We just don’t enforce it.

Dana Lo: TS: we can enforce same in the streaming iterator. If `done=true` and we never saw DONE marker, throw.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: same. We can keep a boolean `sawTerminalEvent` and if loop exits due to io.EOF and false -> error.

Ethan Park: Quick note from platform: gateway guarantees `x-request-id` header on initial response. For mid-stream errors, we can’t always send structured JSON obviously, but request_id should still be available from headers. If SDKs always plumb it through, support can correlate.

Owen Phillips: Support request: please make sure the exception text includes “stream disconnected” and the request_id. Customers paste errors into tickets, we grep.

Ibrahim Khan: Concrete proposal for error shape across SDKs:
- name: `StreamingDisconnectedError`
- fields: `message`, `request_id`, `status_code` (if known; usually 200), `received_event_count`, `received_bytes` (optional), `partial_output` (best effort), `retryable=false` by default
- plus `cause`/`inner` error if available

Allison Grant: re retryable: default false, but maybe `retryable=true` if disconnect happens before first token? That’s likely safe-ish.

Dana Lo: careful: even before first token, server may have started compute. But if it disconnects before any deltas, the customer sees nothing. They might want a retry. We can document: “Retry safe only with idempotency key or when side effects are absent.”

Sabrina James: Agree. The SDK shouldn’t auto-retry a streaming request. But we can expose a helper / doc recipe:
- set idempotency key header
- on `StreamingDisconnectedError`, retry once with same idempotency key

Ibrahim Khan: Another nuance: some customers treat streaming as UI typing effect. Retrying mid-stream can duplicate tokens in UI. We should ensure error includes partial output so they can decide.

Mateusz Zielinski: Implementation note Go: we can accumulate partial text from deltas as we parse. If error triggers, return the partial text on the error.

Dana Lo: TS: same, we can accumulate a `StringBuilder` (or array join) and attach `partial`.

Sabrina James: Python: we can attach `partial_text` to exception. But for chat outputs, might be multiple choices; we can keep partial per choice index.

Ibrahim Khan: Let’s keep it simple for v1: `partial_output` as a JSON-ish string in the error message? But we shouldn’t do nested JSON in the error shape standard. Hmm.

Allison Grant: We can define `partial_output` as “string”, and if multiple choices, join them with separators + mention in docs. The important thing is not losing *something*.

Monica Patel: +1 to typed error and “silent truncation = bug”. Also please make sure we don’t log tokens by default. Partial output should only be on the exception object returned to caller, not in SDK logs.

Sabrina James: Logging: we should log request_id, stream_disconnect_reason, retry_count (0), and whether DONE was seen. But not token content.

Dana Lo: Here’s what I think the TS code path would look like (pseudo):
```ts
let sawDone = false;
let partial = "";
for await (const event of streamSSE(res.body)) {
  if (event.type === "done" || event.data === "[DONE]") { sawDone = true; break; }
  if (event.type === "delta") partial += event.delta;
  yield event;
}
if (!sawDone && !userAborted) {
  throw new StreamingDisconnectedError({ requestId, partialOutput: partial, receivedEventCount });
}
```

Mateusz Zielinski: Go analog:
```go
sawDone := false
partial := strings.Builder{}
for {
  ev, err := dec.Next()
  if err != nil {
    if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) && !sawDone && !ctx.Err() {
      return &StreamingDisconnectedError{RequestID: rid, PartialOutput: partial.String()}
    }
    return err
  }
  if ev.Done { sawDone = true; return nil }
  if ev.Delta != "" { partial.WriteString(ev.Delta) }
  ch <- ev
}
```

Sabrina James: Python sketch:
```py
saw_done = False
partial = []
try:
  for event in _iter_sse(resp):
    if event.is_done:
      saw_done = True
      break
    if event.type == "delta":
      partial.append(event.delta)
    yield event
except Exception as e:
  raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial), cause=e)
finally:
  if not saw_done and not user_cancelled:
    raise StreamingDisconnectedError(request_id=req_id, partial_output="".join(partial))
```
Need to be careful not to double-raise (except/finally). But directionally.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: we should document a “proxy mode” recommendation: set longer read timeout for streaming and disable any default response decompression issues? Some proxies mess with gzip.

Allison Grant: For timeouts: streaming should use “overall = infinite-ish” but “read timeout” should be something like 60s idle. If no bytes for 60s, abort with `StreamIdleTimeoutError` (different from disconnect). That’s another common confusion.

Dana Lo: That’s good. In TS we can implement idle timer that resets on each chunk.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go can use `ReadDeadline` via custom transport / wrapper reader.

Owen Phillips: Customer asked: “Can you just auto-reconnect and continue?” We should have a crisp answer in docs: no resume support; safe retry requires idempotency key; otherwise you may get different outputs / duplicates.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update the parity matrix row for streaming disconnect semantics + propose acceptance criteria
- language owners: implement “DONE required” check + new error type
- Allison: confirm default idle timeout guidance w/ SRE
- Owen: update support macro to ask for request_id + SDK version + whether behind proxy

Ibrahim Khan: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a conformance fixture: server sends 200 + N delta events then closes without DONE. Expected: error type + partial output present + request_id propagated.

Dana Lo: One more edge: user cancellation (AbortController / context cancel) should not be treated as disconnect error. We should map that to `RequestCancelledError`.

Sabrina James: yes. For python, `asyncio.CancelledError` -> cancelled error; and ensure we don’t wrap it as StreamingDisconnected.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go ctx cancellation should return ctx.Err() (or typed wrapper) and not `StreamingDisconnectedError`.

Allison Grant: Please also ensure telemetry field `streaming_disconnect_reason` has controlled values: `eof_before_done`, `idle_timeout`, `user_cancelled`, `parse_error`, `network_error`.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. I’ll draft the standard wording and share in this thread.

Ibrahim Khan: Draft wording for standard (WIP):
- A streaming response is considered successful only if terminal marker is observed (`[DONE]` or equivalent).
- If the transport ends before terminal marker and caller did not cancel, SDK MUST raise `StreamingDisconnectedError` and include `request_id` and best-effort `partial_output`.
- SDK MUST NOT automatically retry streaming requests.
- SDK SHOULD provide guidance for manual retry using idempotency keys.
Feedback welcome.

Monica Patel: Looks right. Make sure it calls out proxies explicitly (common cause) so customers don’t assume it’s always Redwood server-side.

Owen Phillips: +1, and include a troubleshooting blurb: “Try from non-proxied network” / “Ask IT to bypass SSL inspection for api.redwoodinference.com” etc.

Dana Lo: ok from TS side.

Mateusz Zielinski: ok from Go side.

Sabrina James: ok from Python side. I’ll file an issue/PR to enforce DONE semantics + add the error.

Allison Grant: ok. Just ensure we respect privacy around partial output (never log).

source.messages:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick review of retryable status codes + network error classification across SDKs. We’ve got drift between Python (requests/httpx), TS (fetch), Go (net/http). Want to lock a shared table today so conformance tests can encode it.

Ibrahim Khan: Current proposal (non-streaming): retry on 408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 + network/transport errors. Do NOT retry on 400/401/403/404. Open question: 409/423/425? and what we consider a “network error” in each lang.

Sabrina James: +1. In python we currently have a mix:
- requests: `requests.exceptions.Timeout` and `ConnectionError` (includes DNS, refused, reset)
- httpx: `httpx.TimeoutException` vs `httpx.RequestError` (super broad)
Right now we accidentally retry some httpx RequestError cases that are actually local config (bad proxy) and it’s noisy.

Dana Lo: In TS it’s messy because `fetch` throws a `TypeError` for *any* network failure (DNS, refused, CORS-ish, etc). Abort is `AbortError` (DOMException) depending on runtime.
We should treat Abort as NOT retryable (caller canceled / timeout) unless it’s our own internal per-attempt timeout?

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: net/http returns `context deadline exceeded` for timeouts, and `net.Error` w/ Timeout() = true. Also you can get errors when reading the body (EOF, unexpected EOF). Some of those happen after headers were received.
We need to decide whether “read error mid-body” is retryable (esp. if request might have been processed).

Allison Grant: From SRE side, keep retry surface tight. Status codes: 408/429/5xx as listed is good. Please no retries on 409 by default—too many APIs use it as a real conflict.
If platform ever emits a specific error_code for transient lock/overload, we can key off that instead.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 ok, so baseline codes: [408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504].
Question: should we include 520/522 etc (CDN-ish) if they appear? We mostly front w/ our gateway; sometimes customers hit a proxy.

Ethan Park: Gateway shouldn’t emit 520/522, but proxies/CDNs might. I’d rather not encode those unless we see them in support volume. If we do, we should treat unknown 5xx as retryable already.

Sabrina James: “unknown 5xx” => if status >=500 and <=599, retryable except maybe 501? But 501 is “not implemented” and is likely terminal. Still rare.

Dana Lo: Agree. In TS we can do `if (status >= 500 && status !== 501)` as retryable. But to keep parity, we should document exact list vs range?

Allison Grant: Prefer range for 5xx (except 501) so we don’t miss 507/508 etc. But keep the docs explicit about what the SDK does.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok: retryable status predicate:
- status == 408
- status == 429
- status in [500..599] AND status != 501
Anything else => not retry.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works for Go.

Sabrina James: Works for Python.

Dana Lo: Works for TS.

Ibrahim Khan: Now network errors.
My take: retryable if request didn’t definitively reach server OR we didn’t get an HTTP response. That usually means: connect errors, TLS handshake errors, connection reset before headers, DNS, etc.
But if we got a response status, we use the status rules.

Sabrina James: For httpx: if we get `httpx.HTTPStatusError`, it’s a response (don’t treat as network). If we get `httpx.RequestError`, we didn’t get a response. That’s our “network error bucket” (retryable) BUT there are some non-transient cases like invalid URL.
We can filter obvious programmer errors: InvalidURL, UnsupportedProtocol -> not retry.

Dana Lo: JS: no response => fetch rejects. That’s our network bucket. But also CORS can cause fetch rejection in browsers; we don’t officially support browser use, but people do.
Maybe: treat fetch rejection as retryable *only* in Node runtimes? or just retry and let them disable.

Monica Patel: Let’s avoid runtime branching. Default behavior should be consistent. If browser folks complain, we can document “SDKs are server-side; in browsers you should manage retries yourself.”

Dana Lo: ok. For Abort:
- If AbortController triggered because user canceled: not retryable.
- If our internal attempt-timeout triggers an abort: that is retryable (it’s equivalent to per-attempt timeout).
But in practice both look like AbortError unless we tag the signal.

Ibrahim Khan: Suggestion: in TS, wrap attempt timeout abort in a custom error before it bubbles. i.e. map internal timeout to `TimeoutError` (retryable) and map user abort to `AbortError` (not retryable).

Dana Lo: Yep. We can create our own `RequestAbortedError` vs `RequestTimeoutError`.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go has similar: if ctx canceled by user, `context.Canceled` (not retryable). If ctx deadline exceeded due to our attempt timeout, it’s retryable. But we need to ensure we can differentiate “overall timeout reached” vs “attempt timeout hit”.

Allison Grant: Please be careful w/ retrying on overall timeouts. If overall timeout is reached, there should be no further attempts.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Retry loop should stop on overall deadline.

Sabrina James: Python: same. If user sets overall timeout to 10s and we’ve already used 10s, we stop.

Ibrahim Khan: What about read errors / EOF mid-body?
Example: Go streaming or non-streaming where server started response but connection drops.

Mateusz Zielinski: For non-streaming: if we’re reading body and it fails before we parse JSON, we can treat as retryable *if* method is idempotent and no idempotency concerns. But our API calls aren’t strictly idempotent (billing, tokens). So default should be: retryable only if we have not received response headers.

Allison Grant: Strong preference: if headers received, do not auto-retry. It can duplicate completions.

Dana Lo: In TS fetch, you can get a response object then stream read fails. Same issue. So rule: network error before any response headers => retryable. After headers => surface error, no retry.

Sabrina James: That’s implementable. In httpx, RequestError means no response at all; if there’s a response but read fails you’ll get something else (or the stream iterator throws). We can treat streaming separately.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. So network error definition for retry loop:
- Retryable: connect/DNS/TLS/connection reset where NO HTTP response was obtained.
- Not retryable: user cancellation; errors after response headers; invalid URL/config errors.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: ensure we respect `Retry-After` on 429. If present, it overrides computed backoff (capped).

Allison Grant: cap + jitter please. And make sure parsing supports both `Retry-After: 5` and HTTP-date.

Dana Lo: We already parse both in TS; need to align max cap (we currently cap at 60s).

Sabrina James: Python PR in progress; will match cap at 60s too.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go currently ignores Retry-After (oops). Will add.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in—this is good. One more SRE ask: ensure retries on 503 don’t hammer when gateway returns `Retry-After`. During incidents we’ll use that header to slow clients.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 action items:
- I’ll update parity matrix row: retryable status predicate (408,429,5xx!=501), network error definition (no response headers), and “no retry after headers received.”
- Dana: TS mapping for internal timeout abort vs user abort.
- Sabrina: python filter InvalidURL/UnsupportedProtocol as non-retry.
- Mateusz: Go classify ctx.Canceled vs deadline exceeded + ensure no retry after headers.

Monica Patel: Please make sure this lands in conformance fixtures with a few canonical cases:
1) 500 -> retries
2) 501 -> no retry
3) fetch reject (no response) -> retries
4) streaming read disconnect after headers -> no auto retry

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll drop a short snippet in the conformance plan doc + link here when ready.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once you update, tag me and I’ll sanity check for retry storm risk.

Dana Lo: :+1: will push TS changes behind existing retry middleware so it’s not a breaking API.

Sabrina James: 👍

Mateusz Zielinski: 👍

Ibrahim Khan: Also: ensure we respect `Retry-After` on 429. If present, it overrides computed backoff (capped).

Allison Grant: cap + jitter please. And make sure parsing supports both `Retry-After: 5` and HTTP-date.

Dana Lo: We already parse both in TS; need to align max cap (we currently cap at 60s).

Sabrina James: Python PR in progress; will match cap at 60s too.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go currently ignores Retry-After (oops). Will add.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in—this is good. One more SRE ask: ensure retries on 503 don’t hammer when gateway returns `Retry-After`. During incidents we’ll use that header to slow clients.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 action items:
- I’ll update parity matrix row: retryable status predicate (408,429,5xx!=501), network error definition (no response headers), and “no retry after headers received.”
- Dana: TS mapping for internal timeout abort vs user abort.
- Sabrina: python filter InvalidURL/UnsupportedProtocol as non-retry.
- Mateusz: Go classify ctx.Canceled vs deadline exceeded + ensure no retry after headers.

Monica Patel: Please make sure this lands in conformance fixtures with a few canonical cases:
1) 500 -> retries
2) 501 -> no retry
3) fetch reject (no response) -> retries
4) streaming read disconnect after headers -> no auto retry

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll drop a short snippet in the conformance plan doc + link here when ready.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once you update, tag me and I’ll sanity check for retry storm risk.

Dana Lo: :+1: will push TS changes behind existing retry middleware so it’s not a breaking API.

Sabrina James: 👍

Mateusz Zielinski: 👍
text:
Ibrahim Khan: Kicking off a quick review of retryable status codes + network error classification across SDKs. We’ve got drift between Python (requests/httpx), TS (fetch), Go (net/http). Want to lock a shared table today so conformance tests can encode it.

Ibrahim Khan: Current proposal (non-streaming): retry on 408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 + network/transport errors. Do NOT retry on 400/401/403/404. Open question: 409/423/425? and what we consider a “network error” in each lang.

Sabrina James: +1. In python we currently have a mix:
- requests: `requests.exceptions.Timeout` and `ConnectionError` (includes DNS, refused, reset)
- httpx: `httpx.TimeoutException` vs `httpx.RequestError` (super broad)
Right now we accidentally retry some httpx RequestError cases that are actually local config (bad proxy) and it’s noisy.

Dana Lo: In TS it’s messy because `fetch` throws a `TypeError` for *any* network failure (DNS, refused, CORS-ish, etc). Abort is `AbortError` (DOMException) depending on runtime.
We should treat Abort as NOT retryable (caller canceled / timeout) unless it’s our own internal per-attempt timeout?

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: net/http returns `context deadline exceeded` for timeouts, and `net.Error` w/ Timeout() = true. Also you can get errors when reading the body (EOF, unexpected EOF). Some of those happen after headers were received.
We need to decide whether “read error mid-body” is retryable (esp. if request might have been processed).

Allison Grant: From SRE side, keep retry surface tight. Status codes: 408/429/5xx as listed is good. Please no retries on 409 by default—too many APIs use it as a real conflict.
If platform ever emits a specific error_code for transient lock/overload, we can key off that instead.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 ok, so baseline codes: [408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504].
Question: should we include 520/522 etc (CDN-ish) if they appear? We mostly front w/ our gateway; sometimes customers hit a proxy.

Ethan Park: Gateway shouldn’t emit 520/522, but proxies/CDNs might. I’d rather not encode those unless we see them in support volume. If we do, we should treat unknown 5xx as retryable already.

Sabrina James: “unknown 5xx” => if status >=500 and <=599, retryable except maybe 501? But 501 is “not implemented” and is likely terminal. Still rare.

Dana Lo: Agree. In TS we can do `if (status >= 500 && status !== 501)` as retryable. But to keep parity, we should document exact list vs range?

Allison Grant: Prefer range for 5xx (except 501) so we don’t miss 507/508 etc. But keep the docs explicit about what the SDK does.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok: retryable status predicate:
- status == 408
- status == 429
- status in [500..599] AND status != 501
Anything else => not retry.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works for Go.

Sabrina James: Works for Python.

Dana Lo: Works for TS.

Ibrahim Khan: Now network errors.
My take: retryable if request didn’t definitively reach server OR we didn’t get an HTTP response. That usually means: connect errors, TLS handshake errors, connection reset before headers, DNS, etc.
But if we got a response status, we use the status rules.

Sabrina James: For httpx: if we get `httpx.HTTPStatusError`, it’s a response (don’t treat as network). If we get `httpx.RequestError`, we didn’t get a response. That’s our “network error bucket” (retryable) BUT there are some non-transient cases like invalid URL.
We can filter obvious programmer errors: InvalidURL, UnsupportedProtocol -> not retry.

Dana Lo: JS: no response => fetch rejects. That’s our network bucket. But also CORS can cause fetch rejection in browsers; we don’t officially support browser use, but people do.
Maybe: treat fetch rejection as retryable *only* in Node runtimes? or just retry and let them disable.

Monica Patel: Let’s avoid runtime branching. Default behavior should be consistent. If browser folks complain, we can document “SDKs are server-side; in browsers you should manage retries yourself.”

Dana Lo: ok. For Abort:
- If AbortController triggered because user canceled: not retryable.
- If our internal attempt-timeout triggers an abort: that is retryable (it’s equivalent to per-attempt timeout).
But in practice both look like AbortError unless we tag the signal.

Ibrahim Khan: Suggestion: in TS, wrap attempt timeout abort in a custom error before it bubbles. i.e. map internal timeout to `TimeoutError` (retryable) and map user abort to `AbortError` (not retryable).

Dana Lo: Yep. We can create our own `RequestAbortedError` vs `RequestTimeoutError`.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go has similar: if ctx canceled by user, `context.Canceled` (not retryable). If ctx deadline exceeded due to our attempt timeout, it’s retryable. But we need to ensure we can differentiate “overall timeout reached” vs “attempt timeout hit”.

Allison Grant: Please be careful w/ retrying on overall timeouts. If overall timeout is reached, there should be no further attempts.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Retry loop should stop on overall deadline.

Sabrina James: Python: same. If user sets overall timeout to 10s and we’ve already used 10s, we stop.

Ibrahim Khan: What about read errors / EOF mid-body?
Example: Go streaming or non-streaming where server started response but connection drops.

Mateusz Zielinski: For non-streaming: if we’re reading body and it fails before we parse JSON, we can treat as retryable *if* method is idempotent and no idempotency concerns. But our API calls aren’t strictly idempotent (billing, tokens). So default should be: retryable only if we have not received response headers.

Allison Grant: Strong preference: if headers received, do not auto-retry. It can duplicate completions.

Dana Lo: In TS fetch, you can get a response object then stream read fails. Same issue. So rule: network error before any response headers => retryable. After headers => surface error, no retry.

Sabrina James: That’s implementable. In httpx, RequestError means no response at all; if there’s a response but read fails you’ll get something else (or the stream iterator throws). We can treat streaming separately.

Ibrahim Khan: Great. So network error definition for retry loop:
- Retryable: connect/DNS/TLS/connection reset where NO HTTP response was obtained.
- Not retryable: user cancellation; errors after response headers; invalid URL/config errors.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: ensure we respect `Retry-After` on 429. If present, it overrides computed backoff (capped).

Allison Grant: cap + jitter please. And make sure parsing supports both `Retry-After: 5` and HTTP-date.

Dana Lo: We already parse both in TS; need to align max cap (we currently cap at 60s).

Sabrina James: Python PR in progress; will match cap at 60s too.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go currently ignores Retry-After (oops). Will add.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in—this is good. One more SRE ask: ensure retries on 503 don’t hammer when gateway returns `Retry-After`. During incidents we’ll use that header to slow clients.

Ibrahim Khan: 👍 action items:
- I’ll update parity matrix row: retryable status predicate (408,429,5xx!=501), network error definition (no response headers), and “no retry after headers received.”
- Dana: TS mapping for internal timeout abort vs user abort.
- Sabrina: python filter InvalidURL/UnsupportedProtocol as non-retry.
- Mateusz: Go classify ctx.Canceled vs deadline exceeded + ensure no retry after headers.

Monica Patel: Please make sure this lands in conformance fixtures with a few canonical cases:
1) 500 -> retries
2) 501 -> no retry
3) fetch reject (no response) -> retries
4) streaming read disconnect after headers -> no auto retry

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll drop a short snippet in the conformance plan doc + link here when ready.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once you update, tag me and I’ll sanity check for retry storm risk.

Dana Lo: :+1: will push TS changes behind existing retry middleware so it’s not a breaking API.

Sabrina James: 👍

Mateusz Zielinski: 👍

source.text:
Ibrahim Khan: Need to settle SDK default timeouts this week so we can finish ADR + start implementing consistently. Question is overall timeout (single budget) vs connect/read (granular). Also: how do we message the change to existing customers who might be relying on “no timeout” behavior in some libs.

Monica Patel: +1. I keep hearing “Python times out but TS never does” and the opposite for streaming. We need a single story.

Allison Grant: SRE preference: overall timeout is easiest to reason about + prevents hanging sockets forever. But we still need a way to avoid retry storms when customers set it super low.

Dana Lo: In TS/fetch world, we basically have AbortController + a single timer. There isn’t a clean connect vs read split unless we swap HTTP stack (not happening). So “overall” maps well to TS.

Sabrina James: Python’s tricky because httpx exposes `connect/read/write/pool` and people *do* set them. Current SDK behavior is kinda inconsistent (streaming reads can ignore the “overall” if you’re not careful). If we go overall-only, we need a mapping that doesn’t surprise folks.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go has `http.Client.Timeout` (overall) but it’s easy to combine with `Transport` timeouts and context deadlines. For streaming, `http.Client.Timeout` can be problematic because it includes the full body read.

Henry Cho: From PM POV: we need to avoid a breaking change that suddenly makes long generations fail. But also can’t keep “infinite hang” defaults.

Ibrahim Khan: What are we thinking for default values? I’ve seen suggestions: 60s overall for non-streaming, 5s connect, and for streaming a “time to first token” timeout (e.g., 30s) + then no overall limit.

Allison Grant: careful: “no overall limit” on streaming is how we get stuck connections behind proxies. But I get why people want long streams.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in. We should have a default that *terminates* eventually in all modes. If we don’t, oncall gets “SDK hung overnight” tickets and we can’t debug because it’s client side.

Dana Lo: Agree w/ Sean. In TS we already had a few reports where a corporate proxy buffers, the stream never completes, and customers just wait forever. If we default to infinite, that’s guaranteed pain.

Sabrina James: Another angle: a lot of customers “timeout = overall request budget”. If we do connect/read defaults, they won’t know which knob to turn.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: whatever we do, make sure timeout errors don’t print headers. We’ve seen debug logs where people dump request objects.

Monica Patel: Let’s separate: (1) model we implement, (2) defaults, (3) messaging/migration.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Model first. Proposal:
- Expose `timeout_ms` as primary knob across all SDKs (overall budget).
- Also optionally expose granular timeouts per language where it’s idiomatic (`connect_timeout_ms`, `read_timeout_ms`) but keep them advanced/secondary.
- For streaming, treat `timeout_ms` as overall budget by default (including the stream). Optionally allow `stream_idle_timeout_ms` and/or `time_to_first_token_ms`.

Mateusz Zielinski: If `timeout_ms` includes the stream read in Go, that could surprise folks doing long outputs. But it’s consistent.

Sean Gallagher: Consistency > convenience here. If you want a 10-minute stream, set 10 minutes. If you want “no limit”, you should opt in explicitly.

Henry Cho: “Opt in explicitly” is key for messaging: default is safe/finite, power users can override.

Dana Lo: In TS, implementing `stream_idle_timeout_ms` is doable (reset timer on each chunk). That solves proxy buffering / stalled stream without requiring a hard cap on total duration.

Sabrina James: That’s nice. In Python we can do similar: overall cap + idle cap (no bytes received for N ms) + time-to-first-token cap. But defaults need to be careful.

Allison Grant: Defaults suggestion:
- connect timeout: 10s
- time to first byte/token: 30s
- idle timeout (no bytes): 60s
- overall timeout: 10 min for streaming, 60s for non-streaming
But maybe that’s too many.

Monica Patel: that’s already 4 knobs. I’m worried docs will get complicated.

Wei Chen: Docs perspective: we can explain “timeout_ms” and “stream_idle_timeout_ms” as the two knobs. Everything else is internal mapping.

Elliot Price: +1. Keep it to 1-2 knobs in quickstarts. Advanced docs can list the full matrix.

Ibrahim Khan: So maybe:
- `timeout_ms` default = 60_000 for non-streaming; 600_000 for streaming (10 min)
- `stream_idle_timeout_ms` default = 60_000 (only for streaming)
- Keep `connect_timeout_ms` as internal default (10_000) but not part of public surface unless we decide it’s needed.

Dana Lo: Works for TS. `timeout_ms` uses AbortController. `stream_idle_timeout_ms` we implement via resetting a timer when we receive a chunk.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works for Go if we implement idle timeout ourselves via context + reader wrapper, and keep overall via context deadline not `http.Client.Timeout` (because that can behave weirdly with streaming). We can do `context.WithTimeout` for overall and a separate idle timer that cancels.

Sabrina James: Python: can do overall via httpx `Timeout` (or total via `httpx.Timeout(timeout=...)`) and idle via iterating stream and tracking last chunk time. Need tests because httpx has some gotchas.

Allison Grant: From reliability POV I like: only two user-facing knobs + finite defaults. :thumbsup:

Henry Cho: Messaging question: if current TS has effectively “no timeout” in some cases, setting 60s will break long responses.

Monica Patel: Can we detect “streaming request” and default to 10 min there? That reduces breakage.

Dana Lo: Yeah but even non-streaming can be long for huge contexts. 60s might be too low for some customers.

Sean Gallagher: 60s non-streaming seems ok if customers are doing production; if they’re sending giant prompts they should bump it. But we need to avoid sudden failures on upgrade.

Sabrina James: What do we think existing defaults are today?
- Python: I think httpx default is 5s connect + 5s read (?) unless we override, which would explain some “random” timeouts.
- TS: fetch can hang forever unless aborted.
- Go: depends on our client; I think we’ve got no `http.Client.Timeout` set.

Ibrahim Khan: That’s exactly why we need to standardize.

Wei Chen: For the migration guide: we should explicitly say “Previously some SDKs had effectively infinite timeouts; now defaults are finite to prevent hung requests. If you have long-running generations, set `timeout_ms`.” Provide snippets for Python/TS/Go.

Elliot Price: Also add a “Why we changed this” callout: fewer hung requests, consistent behavior across languages, better observability.

Henry Cho: Still worried about the “silent breaking change” vibes. Can we gate the new defaults behind a minor version bump + prominent release notes? Or keep old defaults but emit warnings?

Monica Patel: I’d rather not keep old defaults. But we can do:
- New major/minor version per SDK with migration notes
- Add log/warn on timeout = 0 / None / infinite usage
- Provide a “legacy_timeout_behavior” flag for one release cycle?

Allison Grant: Please no long-lived flags. But a short deprecation window could be okay.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. One cycle max. And default should be safe.

Dana Lo: For TS we can ship as minor with clear changelog + docs. We can also add a console.warn once when timeout is not set and request is streaming >10min? That’s messy.

Sabrina James: Python can emit a warning if users pass deprecated args or if we detect they’re using old env var config (if any). But warning on runtime duration seems bad.

Ibrahim Khan: How about messaging plan:
1) Release notes: “Default timeouts are now finite and consistent; set `timeout_ms` for long requests.”
2) Migration doc: “If you were relying on no timeout, set `timeout_ms: 0` (explicit infinite) OR set large value.”
3) Docs: Recommend not using infinite; if you do, pair with `stream_idle_timeout_ms`.

Sean Gallagher: Please do NOT advertise `timeout_ms: 0` as first-class. That becomes copy/paste and reintroduces hung clients.

Allison Grant: +1. If we allow infinite at all, it should be clearly labeled “not recommended”.

Henry Cho: If we don’t allow infinite, some customers will be unhappy.

Monica Patel: Let’s allow it but only as explicit opt-in and strongly discouraged. Something like `timeout_ms = 0` or `None` depending on language. But docs should not recommend it.

Wei Chen: I can phrase: “Set a higher timeout for long generations. Avoid disabling timeouts; if you must, also configure idle timeouts and cancellation.”

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, `0` duration often means “no timeout”, that’s idiomatic. For TS, maybe `timeoutMs: Infinity` is weird; better `timeoutMs: 0` to mean disabled.

Dana Lo: I’d rather keep TS options numeric and interpret `0` as disabled. We can still keep `stream_idle_timeout_ms` on by default to prevent infinite hangs.

Sabrina James: Python: `timeout=None` is idiomatic. But we can accept `timeout_ms=0` as well for parity.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok so tentative decision:
- Primary: `timeout_ms` overall budget (default 60s non-streaming, 10m streaming)
- Streaming: `stream_idle_timeout_ms` default 60s
- Allow disabling overall timeout only via explicit opt-in (`timeout_ms=0` / `None`), discouraged.
- Docs + migration notes emphasize setting higher values for long generations and avoiding disabling.

Monica Patel: Can we get signoff that this isn’t too many defaults? :eyes:

Sean Gallagher: This is fine. Two knobs. Finite by default. Works.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Also please ensure timeout errors include `request_id` and a `timeout_type` field (overall vs idle) so we can debug.

Ibrahim Khan: yes, will add to error taxonomy.

Henry Cho: From PM side I’m ok if streaming default is 10m and we message clearly. Let’s coordinate release notes so customers don’t think the platform is getting slower.

Wei Chen: I’ll draft migration section:
- “What changed”
- “How to keep previous behavior (not recommended)”
- “Recommended settings for batch/interactive/streaming”

Elliot Price: Link it from the retries/timeouts docs page + quickstarts.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update ADR draft with this model + defaults
- Wei/Elliot: draft migration wording + snippets
- Dana/Mateusz/Sabrina: sanity check that the defaults map cleanly to each HTTP client and don’t break streaming semantics

Dana Lo: :thumbsup:

Sabrina James: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a note re Python httpx mapping (connect/read vs total) so we don’t accidentally end up with “5s read” again.

Mateusz Zielinski: :thumbsup: I’ll verify Go implementation uses context deadline + idle cancel rather than `http.Client.Timeout`.

Ibrahim Khan: Cool. I’ll post ADR link here once updated.

(automated) Workflow Bot: Ibrahim Khan added a link: ADR draft (WIP) - sdk-timeout-model.md

Monica Patel: Can we get signoff that this isn’t too many defaults? :eyes:

Sean Gallagher: This is fine. Two knobs. Finite by default. Works.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Also please ensure timeout errors include `request_id` and a `timeout_type` field (overall vs idle) so we can debug.

Ibrahim Khan: yes, will add to error taxonomy.

Henry Cho: From PM side I’m ok if streaming default is 10m and we message clearly. Let’s coordinate release notes so customers don’t think the platform is getting slower.

Wei Chen: I’ll draft migration section:
- “What changed”
- “How to keep previous behavior (not recommended)”
- “Recommended settings for batch/interactive/streaming”

Elliot Price: Link it from the retries/timeouts docs page + quickstarts.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update ADR draft with this model + defaults
- Wei/Elliot: draft migration wording + snippets
- Dana/Mateusz/Sabrina: sanity check that the defaults map cleanly to each HTTP client and don’t break streaming semantics

Dana Lo: :thumbsup:

Sabrina James: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a note re Python httpx mapping (connect/read vs total) so we don’t accidentally end up with “5s read” again.

Mateusz Zielinski: :thumbsup: I’ll verify Go implementation uses context deadline + idle cancel rather than `http.Client.Timeout`.

Ibrahim Khan: Cool. I’ll post ADR link here once updated.

text:
Ibrahim Khan: Need to settle SDK default timeouts this week so we can finish ADR + start implementing consistently. Question is overall timeout (single budget) vs connect/read (granular). Also: how do we message the change to existing customers who might be relying on “no timeout” behavior in some libs.

Monica Patel: +1. I keep hearing “Python times out but TS never does” and the opposite for streaming. We need a single story.

Allison Grant: SRE preference: overall timeout is easiest to reason about + prevents hanging sockets forever. But we still need a way to avoid retry storms when customers set it super low.

Dana Lo: In TS/fetch world, we basically have AbortController + a single timer. There isn’t a clean connect vs read split unless we swap HTTP stack (not happening). So “overall” maps well to TS.

Sabrina James: Python’s tricky because httpx exposes `connect/read/write/pool` and people *do* set them. Current SDK behavior is kinda inconsistent (streaming reads can ignore the “overall” if you’re not careful). If we go overall-only, we need a mapping that doesn’t surprise folks.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go has `http.Client.Timeout` (overall) but it’s easy to combine with `Transport` timeouts and context deadlines. For streaming, `http.Client.Timeout` can be problematic because it includes the full body read.

Henry Cho: From PM POV: we need to avoid a breaking change that suddenly makes long generations fail. But also can’t keep “infinite hang” defaults.

Ibrahim Khan: What are we thinking for default values? I’ve seen suggestions: 60s overall for non-streaming, 5s connect, and for streaming a “time to first token” timeout (e.g., 30s) + then no overall limit.

Allison Grant: careful: “no overall limit” on streaming is how we get stuck connections behind proxies. But I get why people want long streams.

Sean Gallagher: Jumping in. We should have a default that *terminates* eventually in all modes. If we don’t, oncall gets “SDK hung overnight” tickets and we can’t debug because it’s client side.

Dana Lo: Agree w/ Sean. In TS we already had a few reports where a corporate proxy buffers, the stream never completes, and customers just wait forever. If we default to infinite, that’s guaranteed pain.

Sabrina James: Another angle: a lot of customers “timeout = overall request budget”. If we do connect/read defaults, they won’t know which knob to turn.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: whatever we do, make sure timeout errors don’t print headers. We’ve seen debug logs where people dump request objects.

Monica Patel: Let’s separate: (1) model we implement, (2) defaults, (3) messaging/migration.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Model first. Proposal:
- Expose `timeout_ms` as primary knob across all SDKs (overall budget).
- Also optionally expose granular timeouts per language where it’s idiomatic (`connect_timeout_ms`, `read_timeout_ms`) but keep them advanced/secondary.
- For streaming, treat `timeout_ms` as overall budget by default (including the stream). Optionally allow `stream_idle_timeout_ms` and/or `time_to_first_token_ms`.

Mateusz Zielinski: If `timeout_ms` includes the stream read in Go, that could surprise folks doing long outputs. But it’s consistent.

Sean Gallagher: Consistency > convenience here. If you want a 10-minute stream, set 10 minutes. If you want “no limit”, you should opt in explicitly.

Henry Cho: “Opt in explicitly” is key for messaging: default is safe/finite, power users can override.

Dana Lo: In TS, implementing `stream_idle_timeout_ms` is doable (reset timer on each chunk). That solves proxy buffering / stalled stream without requiring a hard cap on total duration.

Sabrina James: That’s nice. In Python we can do similar: overall cap + idle cap (no bytes received for N ms) + time-to-first-token cap. But defaults need to be careful.

Allison Grant: Defaults suggestion:
- connect timeout: 10s
- time to first byte/token: 30s
- idle timeout (no bytes): 60s
- overall timeout: 10 min for streaming, 60s for non-streaming
But maybe that’s too many.

Monica Patel: that’s already 4 knobs. I’m worried docs will get complicated.

Wei Chen: Docs perspective: we can explain “timeout_ms” and “stream_idle_timeout_ms” as the two knobs. Everything else is internal mapping.

Elliot Price: +1. Keep it to 1-2 knobs in quickstarts. Advanced docs can list the full matrix.

Ibrahim Khan: So maybe:
- `timeout_ms` default = 60_000 for non-streaming; 600_000 for streaming (10 min)
- `stream_idle_timeout_ms` default = 60_000 (only for streaming)
- Keep `connect_timeout_ms` as internal default (10_000) but not part of public surface unless we decide it’s needed.

Dana Lo: Works for TS. `timeout_ms` uses AbortController. `stream_idle_timeout_ms` we implement via resetting a timer when we receive a chunk.

Mateusz Zielinski: Works for Go if we implement idle timeout ourselves via context + reader wrapper, and keep overall via context deadline not `http.Client.Timeout` (because that can behave weirdly with streaming). We can do `context.WithTimeout` for overall and a separate idle timer that cancels.

Sabrina James: Python: can do overall via httpx `Timeout` (or total via `httpx.Timeout(timeout=...)`) and idle via iterating stream and tracking last chunk time. Need tests because httpx has some gotchas.

Allison Grant: From reliability POV I like: only two user-facing knobs + finite defaults. :thumbsup:

Henry Cho: Messaging question: if current TS has effectively “no timeout” in some cases, setting 60s will break long responses.

Monica Patel: Can we detect “streaming request” and default to 10 min there? That reduces breakage.

Dana Lo: Yeah but even non-streaming can be long for huge contexts. 60s might be too low for some customers.

Sean Gallagher: 60s non-streaming seems ok if customers are doing production; if they’re sending giant prompts they should bump it. But we need to avoid sudden failures on upgrade.

Sabrina James: What do we think existing defaults are today?
- Python: I think httpx default is 5s connect + 5s read (?) unless we override, which would explain some “random” timeouts.
- TS: fetch can hang forever unless aborted.
- Go: depends on our client; I think we’ve got no `http.Client.Timeout` set.

Ibrahim Khan: That’s exactly why we need to standardize.

Wei Chen: For the migration guide: we should explicitly say “Previously some SDKs had effectively infinite timeouts; now defaults are finite to prevent hung requests. If you have long-running generations, set `timeout_ms`.” Provide snippets for Python/TS/Go.

Elliot Price: Also add a “Why we changed this” callout: fewer hung requests, consistent behavior across languages, better observability.

Henry Cho: Still worried about the “silent breaking change” vibes. Can we gate the new defaults behind a minor version bump + prominent release notes? Or keep old defaults but emit warnings?

Monica Patel: I’d rather not keep old defaults. But we can do:
- New major/minor version per SDK with migration notes
- Add log/warn on timeout = 0 / None / infinite usage
- Provide a “legacy_timeout_behavior” flag for one release cycle?

Allison Grant: Please no long-lived flags. But a short deprecation window could be okay.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. One cycle max. And default should be safe.

Dana Lo: For TS we can ship as minor with clear changelog + docs. We can also add a console.warn once when timeout is not set and request is streaming >10min? That’s messy.

Sabrina James: Python can emit a warning if users pass deprecated args or if we detect they’re using old env var config (if any). But warning on runtime duration seems bad.

Ibrahim Khan: How about messaging plan:
1) Release notes: “Default timeouts are now finite and consistent; set `timeout_ms` for long requests.”
2) Migration doc: “If you were relying on no timeout, set `timeout_ms: 0` (explicit infinite) OR set large value.”
3) Docs: Recommend not using infinite; if you do, pair with `stream_idle_timeout_ms`.

Sean Gallagher: Please do NOT advertise `timeout_ms: 0` as first-class. That becomes copy/paste and reintroduces hung clients.

Allison Grant: +1. If we allow infinite at all, it should be clearly labeled “not recommended”.

Henry Cho: If we don’t allow infinite, some customers will be unhappy.

Monica Patel: Let’s allow it but only as explicit opt-in and strongly discouraged. Something like `timeout_ms = 0` or `None` depending on language. But docs should not recommend it.

Wei Chen: I can phrase: “Set a higher timeout for long generations. Avoid disabling timeouts; if you must, also configure idle timeouts and cancellation.”

Mateusz Zielinski: For Go, `0` duration often means “no timeout”, that’s idiomatic. For TS, maybe `timeoutMs: Infinity` is weird; better `timeoutMs: 0` to mean disabled.

Dana Lo: I’d rather keep TS options numeric and interpret `0` as disabled. We can still keep `stream_idle_timeout_ms` on by default to prevent infinite hangs.

Sabrina James: Python: `timeout=None` is idiomatic. But we can accept `timeout_ms=0` as well for parity.

Ibrahim Khan: Ok so tentative decision:
- Primary: `timeout_ms` overall budget (default 60s non-streaming, 10m streaming)
- Streaming: `stream_idle_timeout_ms` default 60s
- Allow disabling overall timeout only via explicit opt-in (`timeout_ms=0` / `None`), discouraged.
- Docs + migration notes emphasize setting higher values for long generations and avoiding disabling.

Monica Patel: Can we get signoff that this isn’t too many defaults? :eyes:

Sean Gallagher: This is fine. Two knobs. Finite by default. Works.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Also please ensure timeout errors include `request_id` and a `timeout_type` field (overall vs idle) so we can debug.

Ibrahim Khan: yes, will add to error taxonomy.

Henry Cho: From PM side I’m ok if streaming default is 10m and we message clearly. Let’s coordinate release notes so customers don’t think the platform is getting slower.

Wei Chen: I’ll draft migration section:
- “What changed”
- “How to keep previous behavior (not recommended)”
- “Recommended settings for batch/interactive/streaming”

Elliot Price: Link it from the retries/timeouts docs page + quickstarts.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: update ADR draft with this model + defaults
- Wei/Elliot: draft migration wording + snippets
- Dana/Mateusz/Sabrina: sanity check that the defaults map cleanly to each HTTP client and don’t break streaming semantics

Dana Lo: :thumbsup:

Sabrina James: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a note re Python httpx mapping (connect/read vs total) so we don’t accidentally end up with “5s read” again.

Mateusz Zielinski: :thumbsup: I’ll verify Go implementation uses context deadline + idle cancel rather than `http.Client.Timeout`.

Ibrahim Khan: Cool. I’ll post ADR link here once updated.

source.thread:
Monica Patel: Kicking off SDK parity initiative (auth/retries/timeouts/streaming). Goal is to stop “works in TS but not Python” support churn. Owners: TS @Dana Lo, Go @Mateusz Zielinski, Python/examples @Sabrina James. Tech lead @Ibrahim Khan. 

Monica Patel: Key docs:
- Parity audit overview (Confluence): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGDEVEX/SDK+Parity+Audit+Overview
- Working notes (Drive): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/sdk-parity-audit-working-notes
- Parity matrix (Sheet): https://drive.google.com/spreadsheets/d/sdk-parity-matrix

Monica Patel: Please reply here with (1) any known inconsistencies you’ve seen recently, (2) where the code diverges, (3) any “must not break” behaviors for your SDK. We’ll use this to seed the matrix + initial set of conformance tests.

Ibrahim Khan: :wave: I’ll run point on the standard + conformance suite. I’m creating the initial matrix tab today (auth/retry/timeout/streaming). If you have existing issues/PRs/links, drop them here.

Dana Lo: TS knowns:
- We currently retry some 4xx we shouldn’t (I’ve seen 400 retry on a customer repro… might be coming from the generic fetch middleware). 
- Timeout behavior is inconsistent between streaming vs non-streaming (AbortController wired for non-streaming, streaming path sometimes just hangs).
- Env vars: we read `REDWOOD_API_KEY` but some docs/examples still mention `REDWOOD_TOKEN` (legacy).

Dana Lo: “Must not break”: existing users passing `apiKey` explicitly in client constructor should still override env vars.

Sabrina James: Python knowns:
- Streaming timeouts not honored (we set timeout on request but the iterator can block on read indefinitely depending on http client settings). Support ticket exists.
- Error typing: sometimes we raise a generic Exception w/o request_id, esp. on stream disconnects.
- Retry-After: we parse seconds but date format is flaky / not implemented (I need to confirm).

Sabrina James: Must not break: keep `REDWOOD_API_KEY` env support + existing `timeout=` arg (even if we migrate to connect/read/overall under the hood). We can deprecate but not yank.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go knowns:
- We don’t currently respect Retry-After on 429 (we just backoff with fixed sleep). This makes us too aggressive during rate limiting.
- Context cancellation works for request start, but I’ve seen streaming reads continue for a bit after ctx cancel (net/http body read loop not checking ctx early enough).
- Auth env naming is inconsistent vs TS/Python; I’ve seen `REDWOOD_API_KEY` in code but docs mention `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` in one place.

Mateusz Zielinski: Must not break: default client should stay “simple” (no required config). But we can add options for retries/timeouts.

Allison Grant: From SRE angle, biggest pain is “retry storms” when we’re returning elevated 429s. We need consistent retryable codes + max attempts + jitter.

Allison Grant: Known inconsistencies (from incident review):
- Some SDKs retry immediately on 429 without honoring `Retry-After`.
- Some retry on 5xx w/ no jitter => thundering herd.
- Some treat DNS/timeouts differently (TS fetch vs Go net/http vs Python httpx).

Allison Grant: Please make sure we standardize emitted logging fields: request_id, retry_count, attempt, timeout_ms, and streaming_disconnect_reason. Even if it’s just via hooks.

Marcus Lin: Security notes for kickoff:
- Auth precedence must be consistent and documented (explicit param > env vars).
- Env vars: prefer one canonical name, deprecate others w/ warnings.
- Never include raw secrets in logs/errors. If you add debug logging, must redact.

Marcus Lin: Also: if we introduce “bearer token” support, ensure header formatting is always `Authorization: Bearer <token>` and errors don’t echo token values.

Elliot Price: Docs side: we’ll need a single canonical set of snippets for all three languages. Please tag me when the final option names are locked.

Wei Chen: +1. Also would love a short “migration notes” list once we know what defaults change (timeouts, retry max attempts). Customers get spooked if it’s surprising.

Sahana Iyer: For conformance tests: where do we want the fixtures to live? Separate repo vs copy into each SDK repo? CI wise we can run a dockerized “fake gateway” and assert retry/timeout behavior. 

Ibrahim Khan: My proposal: shared fixture definitions in a small repo or a vendored folder copied across (to keep CI simple). We can start by defining golden cases:
1) 429 with Retry-After seconds
2) 429 with Retry-After HTTP date
3) 500/502/503/504 retries w/ jitter
4) network error (connection reset / timeout)
5) streaming disconnect mid-chunk (no auto-retry; surface partial output + typed error)

Ibrahim Khan: Re: known issues, thanks. I’m adding them into the matrix under “current behavior” columns.

Connor O'Brien: For release coordination: please aim for feature flags / opt-in where we think behavior change is significant (esp. timeouts + retries). We can do staged minor releases + a coordinated announcement.

Dana Lo: Question: do we have a current “standard” doc for retryable status codes? I’ve seen: TS retries 429 + 5xx + some fetch failures, but not consistent.

Allison Grant: Not yet published, but I can draft the recommended set. Generally: retry on 429, 408, 409 (maybe), and 5xx (except some cases), plus network errors. But streaming should be conservative.

Sabrina James: For Python, we should explicitly NOT retry 400/401/403/404. And for 409, depends if it’s safe/idempotent.

Mateusz Zielinski: Agree. For Go we can make 409 opt-in (or only if idempotency key is present?). Otherwise it’s scary.

Ibrahim Khan: Action items:
- Everyone: add any remaining “mismatch” bullets to this thread by EOD tomorrow.
- Ibrahim: seed matrix + draft standard proposal (auth precedence, retry policy, timeout model, streaming semantics).
- Language owners: identify current defaults (timeout values, retry attempts/backoff) and where they’re set in code.

Ibrahim Khan: Also: please sanity check env var names your SDK reads today. I suspect we have at least 3 variants floating around.

Linear Bot: ENG-5120 “SDK parity audit (auth/retry/timeout/streaming)” created by Monica Patel and assigned to Ibrahim Khan. https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-5120

Dana Lo: I’ll dig up the middleware location + current retry list in TS and paste in working notes.

Sabrina James: I’ll link the support ticket + repro for streaming timeout hang. Also will confirm Retry-After parsing behavior.

Mateusz Zielinski: I’ll open a Go sub-issue for Retry-After + ctx cancel on streaming read loop.

Monica Patel: Thanks all. Keep updates in this thread for kickoff week; we’ll move to the matrix + Linear sub-issues after we have the first pass. :thumbsup:

source.text:
Maya Singh: Hey folks — landing my first tiny PR to bump a handful of deps in package-builder. quick questions: branch naming, commit format, and any repo-specific checks I should run?

Ethan Park: Welcome Maya! Branches: <type>/<linear>-short-desc or <type>/<ticket>-short. We use chore for deps. e.g. chore/4287-bump-psutil.

Maya Singh: Good to know. Commit message style? Do we keep multiple commits or squash?

Helen (eng): We prefer a single clean commit for dependency bumps. Use the conventional style: chore(deps): bump psutil 5.8.0 -> 5.9.1. If there were notable behavior changes add a short body.

Maya Singh: Where's the PR template and label guidance?

Ethan Park: PR template: .github/pull_request_template.md. Add labels: area/build and label:needs-review. For dep bumps include a migration/compat note if needed.

ProtoBot: FYI: If you modify any proto files, update generated files under proto-gen and run `scripts/gen_protos.sh`.

Maya Singh: Thanks — no protos here. CI failed earlier on my branch with a license header complaint even though pre-commit passed locally. Any common root cause?

Helen (eng): License header checks are enforced by pre-commit hook but CI uses an older hook cache sometimes. Run `pre-commit run license-header --all-files` or `scripts/apply_license_headers`. Also ensure your editor didn't strip the comment start.

Maya Singh: Got it. What about mypy/untyped code? I see occasional CI mypy failures for other PRs.

Ethan Park: We run mypy in CI with strict config for certain packages. Prefer adding narrow typing or # type: ignore with a short comment if a third-party stubs are missing. Locally run `tox -e py39-mypy` to match CI.

Maya Singh: Any perf checks for build-related changes?

Luis: For packaging/deps, not usually. If you touch runtime libs (numpy, torch) ping @luis and run the quick smoke harness scripts/bench/smoke.sh.

Maya Singh: Good point — none of those here. Pre-merge checklist I should follow?

Ethan Park: Quick checklist: 1) run pre-commit and tests 2) make sure changelog (if user-facing) is updated 3) link Linear ticket in PR body 4) add reviewers (owner + one peer)

Maya Singh: Who are the owners for package-builder?

Helen (eng): Tag @ethan and @helen. Owner files are in CODEOWNERS. If unsure, request an informal review from the oncall.

CI Bot: build/1751239876 — status: running (lint, unit tests, mypy) :hourglass:

Maya Singh: One small nit — do we prefer rebasing onto main before merge?

Ethan Park: Yes, rebase to keep main linear. If there are merge conflicts, resolve on your branch and re-run CI.

Maya Singh: I bumped three transitive deps in subpackage. Anything else I should include in the PR body?

Helen (eng): Add a short testing note: which tests you ran locally and CI artifacts to watch for. Also mark the PR as non-breaking if applicable.

CI Bot: build/1751239876 — status: success (lint, unit tests) :white_check_mark:

Maya Singh: All green. Sending for review now — thanks for the quick onboarding help! :tada:

Ethan Park: Welcome aboard Maya, and congrats on the first merged PR. Small thing — after merge, run `make verify-ci` once to ensure branch protection didn't block anything.

Maya Singh: Will do — thanks everyone!

source.text:
Talia Benson: Hey all — pushing the launch brief into "ready to execute" today. Need final owner assignments + dates for the remaining launch tasks so I can lock the checklist + comms. Please reply in-thread with what you own + ETA.

Scope reminders for v1:
- Python + TS RAG+agent templates
- CLI: `init` + `doctor` (no in-place upgrade story for v1)
- observability pack dashboards + recommended alerts
- docs IA + walkthrough + troubleshooting
- support enablement + announcement

Owner buckets we need to nail:
1) Docs updates (IA/nav, walkthrough, troubleshooting)
2) Dashboard pack (publish + verify)
3) Template release (repo tags, changelog, version stamps)
4) Support enablement (runbook, FAQ, escalation route)
5) Announcement (internal + external)
