source.messages:
Monica Patel: Pulling together a list of SDK gaps that are actively blocking the quickstart templates (Python + TS) from feeling “production-minded by default”. Dropping here so we can track what’s must-fix before launch vs nice-to-have. Please add anything I missed.

Monica Patel: **TS SDK**
1) Streaming error types are too lossy. In templates we can’t reliably tell `timeout` vs `connection reset` vs `429` vs `5xx` during stream. Right now it’s basically `Error: fetch failed` and we lose status/code.
2) No clean “stream aborted by client” vs “server closed stream” distinction.
3) Reconnect strategy for streaming: we need a safe default pattern (e.g., retry on network disconnect before any tokens, but do not replay partial responses). SDK doesn’t expose hooks/metadata to do this sanely.
4) Lack of a stable request-id capture. We need Redwood request id (gateway header) to show up in logs/traces.

Monica Patel: **Python SDK**
1) Trace context propagation helpers are missing / awkward. Template instrumentation currently has to do custom glue to attach span context + request-id.
2) Timeout + retry defaults are not aligned with the safe-defaults doc (templates are doing overrides). Ideally SDK defaults match so templates don’t carry bespoke policy.
3) Streaming: when the generator errors mid-stream, we need better exception typing + access to response metadata (status, headers) for logging and support.

Monica Patel: **Cross-cutting**
- OTel-friendly hooks: we need a supported way to wrap client calls so templates can emit spans without monkeypatching.
- Template telemetry tags/headers: if we’re sending an opt-in header, SDK should preserve it and not drop/rename it. (Also need gateway whitelist; Brian/Logan tracking?)

Dana Lo: +1 on TS streaming. In the template I’m trying to do something like:
```ts
for await (const chunk of client.chat.completions.stream(...)) {
  // ...
}
```
When it blows up, it’s not actionable. We need `err.status`, `err.code`, and ideally `err.retryable`.

Ibrahim Khan: For Python, we can land helpers similar to what we did in other clients:
- `get_current_traceparent()` (or equivalent)
- capture `x-redwood-request-id` from responses
- structured errors: `AuthError`, `QuotaError`, `RateLimitError`, `NetworkError`, `TimeoutError`, `ServerError`
Then templates can map those to our error taxonomy + log fields.

Sabrina James: On tests side, missing error typing makes it hard to write CI smoke tests. Right now the streaming tests can’t assert “we got a 429 and backed off” vs “socket hung up”. Everything looks the same.

Brian Murphy: Re: request id + headers — gateway currently returns `x-redwood-request-id` consistently, but not all SDKs surface it. TS: fetch Response headers are there but we throw away the Response object. If we refactor the internals to keep `response` around, we can expose requestId. Same for Python.

Allison Grant: From observability POV, templates need:
- span attributes: `redwood.request_id`, `llm.provider`, `llm.model`, `llm.streaming=true/false`, `http.status_code`
- stable error classification for `error.type`
Right now templates are guessing. If SDK gives typed errors + request id, we can standardize.

Wei Chen: Adding doc pain: troubleshooting page needs to say “if you see X, do Y”. Without typed errors we can’t give deterministic guidance.

Logan Wright: Telemetry tagging: we need to be careful with headers. Some corp proxies reject unknown headers (we’ve seen this elsewhere). If templates are going to tag traffic, it should be **opt-in** and (ideally) via query param or a whitelisted header with a very conservative format.

Monica Patel: Totally. For launch I think we do opt-in only and default OFF. But even opt-in we need SDK to pass it through. Dana: are you using a custom header or metadata field?

Dana Lo: Currently passing a header like `x-redwood-template: rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` (example). But our internal client wrapper doesn’t consistently include it across calls, esp. the streaming path.

Brian Murphy: That specific header will need whitelist validation on gateway. Also, format: `rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` is fine, but we should restrict chars to avoid injection. Logan can advise.

Ibrahim Khan: For retries/backoff: templates currently implement their own. That’s risky. If we can align SDK defaults with safe defaults (timeouts, max attempts, jitter) we reduce drift + support load.

Monica Patel: Agree. Proposal for “must-fix before template launch” (v1):
- TS: streaming reconnect + error typing + request-id extraction
- Python: context propagation helpers + request-id extraction + retry/timeout defaults
- Both: OTel hooks (official way) so templates don’t patch internals

Sabrina James: Also: TS abort behavior. When user closes the HTTP connection to our template server, we should abort the upstream LLM call. Today we call `AbortController.abort()` but the SDK doesn’t always stop the stream; we leak work.

Dana Lo: Yes, that’s a big one. In Node, `fetch` / undici semantics are weird. We need the SDK to treat abort as first-class and stop reading/decoding.

Allison Grant: +1. Also would be good if “abort” is not logged as an error in traces. Otherwise dashboards show false incident spikes.

Ibrahim Khan: We can model abort as a distinct exception type and mark span status appropriately (`UNSET` or `OK` depending on conventions). We should align with OTel semantic convs.

Logan Wright: Quick note: attribute cardinality. Please do not add raw error messages or user IDs as span attributes. Request id is fine. Model name is fine. Any “template version” tag should be bounded.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: can you write up a concrete Python SDK change list (APIs + error classes + request id surfacing) by EOW?
- Dana: can you open a TS SDK issue with a minimal reproduction for streaming errors + abort leaks?
- Brian/Logan: confirm header whitelist + recommended format if we keep template tagging opt-in.
- Allison: share the “minimum required attributes” list for templates so SDK hooks can set them consistently.

Dana Lo: Yep. I’ll file it and link to the template PR where we’re working around it.

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll also cross-check with the safe-defaults doc so we’re not inventing a new policy.

Brian Murphy: I’ll sync with gateway folks about whitelist constraints and whether we can do a query param instead of header.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop the attribute list + do/don’t for cardinality in this thread.

Wei Chen: Once we know the final error shapes, I can update troubleshooting copy with exact messages + remediation steps.

Monica Patel: Perfect. Goal is to stop papering over SDK gaps in templates. Templates should be thin glue + best practices, not a second SDK.

Ibrahim Khan: For Python, we can land helpers similar to what we did in other clients:
- `get_current_traceparent()` (or equivalent)
- capture `x-redwood-request-id` from responses
- structured errors: `AuthError`, `QuotaError`, `RateLimitError`, `NetworkError`, `TimeoutError`, `ServerError`
Then templates can map those to our error taxonomy + log fields.

Sabrina James: On tests side, missing error typing makes it hard to write CI smoke tests. Right now the streaming tests can’t assert “we got a 429 and backed off” vs “socket hung up”. Everything looks the same.

Brian Murphy: Re: request id + headers — gateway currently returns `x-redwood-request-id` consistently, but not all SDKs surface it. TS: fetch Response headers are there but we throw away the Response object. If we refactor the internals to keep `response` around, we can expose requestId. Same for Python.

Allison Grant: From observability POV, templates need:
- span attributes: `redwood.request_id`, `llm.provider`, `llm.model`, `llm.streaming=true/false`, `http.status_code`
- stable error classification for `error.type`
Right now templates are guessing. If SDK gives typed errors + request id, we can standardize.

Wei Chen: Adding doc pain: troubleshooting page needs to say “if you see X, do Y”. Without typed errors we can’t give deterministic guidance.

Logan Wright: Telemetry tagging: we need to be careful with headers. Some corp proxies reject unknown headers (we’ve seen this elsewhere). If templates are going to tag traffic, it should be **opt-in** and (ideally) via query param or a whitelisted header with a very conservative format.

Monica Patel: Totally. For launch I think we do opt-in only and default OFF. But even opt-in we need SDK to pass it through. Dana: are you using a custom header or metadata field?

Dana Lo: Currently passing a header like `x-redwood-template: rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` (example). But our internal client wrapper doesn’t consistently include it across calls, esp. the streaming path.

Brian Murphy: That specific header will need whitelist validation on gateway. Also, format: `rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` is fine, but we should restrict chars to avoid injection. Logan can advise.

Ibrahim Khan: For retries/backoff: templates currently implement their own. That’s risky. If we can align SDK defaults with safe defaults (timeouts, max attempts, jitter) we reduce drift + support load.

Monica Patel: Agree. Proposal for “must-fix before template launch” (v1):
- TS: streaming reconnect + error typing + request-id extraction
- Python: context propagation helpers + request-id extraction + retry/timeout defaults
- Both: OTel hooks (official way) so templates don’t patch internals

Sabrina James: Also: TS abort behavior. When user closes the HTTP connection to our template server, we should abort the upstream LLM call. Today we call `AbortController.abort()` but the SDK doesn’t always stop the stream; we leak work.

Dana Lo: Yes, that’s a big one. In Node, `fetch` / undici semantics are weird. We need the SDK to treat abort as first-class and stop reading/decoding.

Allison Grant: +1. Also would be good if “abort” is not logged as an error in traces. Otherwise dashboards show false incident spikes.

Ibrahim Khan: We can model abort as a distinct exception type and mark span status appropriately (`UNSET` or `OK` depending on conventions). We should align with OTel semantic convs.

Logan Wright: Quick note: attribute cardinality. Please do not add raw error messages or user IDs as span attributes. Request id is fine. Model name is fine. Any “template version” tag should be bounded.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: can you write up a concrete Python SDK change list (APIs + error classes + request id surfacing) by EOW?
- Dana: can you open a TS SDK issue with a minimal reproduction for streaming errors + abort leaks?
- Brian/Logan: confirm header whitelist + recommended format if we keep template tagging opt-in.
- Allison: share the “minimum required attributes” list for templates so SDK hooks can set them consistently.

Dana Lo: Yep. I’ll file it and link to the template PR where we’re working around it.

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll also cross-check with the safe-defaults doc so we’re not inventing a new policy.

Brian Murphy: I’ll sync with gateway folks about whitelist constraints and whether we can do a query param instead of header.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop the attribute list + do/don’t for cardinality in this thread.

Wei Chen: Once we know the final error shapes, I can update troubleshooting copy with exact messages + remediation steps.

Monica Patel: Perfect. Goal is to stop papering over SDK gaps in templates. Templates should be thin glue + best practices, not a second SDK.
text:
Monica Patel: Pulling together a list of SDK gaps that are actively blocking the quickstart templates (Python + TS) from feeling “production-minded by default”. Dropping here so we can track what’s must-fix before launch vs nice-to-have. Please add anything I missed.

Monica Patel: **TS SDK**
1) Streaming error types are too lossy. In templates we can’t reliably tell `timeout` vs `connection reset` vs `429` vs `5xx` during stream. Right now it’s basically `Error: fetch failed` and we lose status/code.
2) No clean “stream aborted by client” vs “server closed stream” distinction.
3) Reconnect strategy for streaming: we need a safe default pattern (e.g., retry on network disconnect before any tokens, but do not replay partial responses). SDK doesn’t expose hooks/metadata to do this sanely.
4) Lack of a stable request-id capture. We need Redwood request id (gateway header) to show up in logs/traces.

Monica Patel: **Python SDK**
1) Trace context propagation helpers are missing / awkward. Template instrumentation currently has to do custom glue to attach span context + request-id.
2) Timeout + retry defaults are not aligned with the safe-defaults doc (templates are doing overrides). Ideally SDK defaults match so templates don’t carry bespoke policy.
3) Streaming: when the generator errors mid-stream, we need better exception typing + access to response metadata (status, headers) for logging and support.

Monica Patel: **Cross-cutting**
- OTel-friendly hooks: we need a supported way to wrap client calls so templates can emit spans without monkeypatching.
- Template telemetry tags/headers: if we’re sending an opt-in header, SDK should preserve it and not drop/rename it. (Also need gateway whitelist; Brian/Logan tracking?)

Dana Lo: +1 on TS streaming. In the template I’m trying to do something like:
```ts
for await (const chunk of client.chat.completions.stream(...)) {
  // ...
}
```
When it blows up, it’s not actionable. We need `err.status`, `err.code`, and ideally `err.retryable`.

- `get_current_traceparent()` (or equivalent)

source.messages:
Monica Patel: Kicking off a thread to track security review open items for Quickstart Templates (RAG+agents). Want to close these before we call internal beta “ready”. Please drop issues + owner + ETA here.

Marcus Lin: Thx. From the security pass today, top open items:
1) Secrets in logs: a couple places where exceptions may print request headers/resp bodies. Need explicit redaction + test.
2) Sample data: current docs/sample corpus includes “realistic” customer-like content. We need it clearly synthetic + no PII. Also verify license/attribution.
3) Dependency pinning: Python + TS templates still allow floating ranges in a few deps. Need lockfiles + CI to enforce.
4) Redaction processors: OTel collector config needs processors to drop/redact sensitive attrs (headers, prompt content, tool args) by default.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 on (1). In the Python template I saw `logger.exception("request failed", extra={"headers": headers})` in one retry path. If `Authorization` ever gets into that dict we’re toast.
Suggestion: redaction helper that deep-copies dict, strips `authorization`, `x-api-key`, `cookie`, and also anything matching `*_KEY`, `*_TOKEN`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Also check env var naming + docs. We should consistently use `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and never encourage putting keys in `.env` that gets committed.
README should say:
- copy `.env.example` -> `.env` (gitignored)
- use `direnv`/platform secrets manager for deploy
- never paste keys into logs/issues

Dana Lo: TS template: I added structured logging but we’re still doing `console.error(err)` in a couple places. Some errors contain request config which can include headers.
I can swap to a `safeError(err)` serializer that strips `config.headers` and truncates `response.data`.

Allison Grant: On OTel: we should treat traces/metrics/logs as potentially egressing to vendor backends.
Minimum:
- do not emit prompt content by default
- do not emit tool inputs/outputs by default
- include request id + model + status + latency + token counts ok
For local collector compose, add attribute processor rules to delete anything like `http.request.header.authorization` and `http.request.header.cookie`.

Sahana Iyer: Dependency pinning + scanning: for TS we should enforce `pnpm-lock.yaml` (or npm lock) and fail CI if it changes unexpectedly. For Python, `uv lock` or `pip-tools` lockfile.
I can add workflows:
- `dependency-review` (GitHub)
- `pip-audit` / `npm audit` (with allowlist for known false positives)
- `gitleaks` scanning

Wei Chen: Sample data: I can rewrite the sample docs to be clearly synthetic (e.g., Redwood product FAQs) and add a note in the README.
Right now it’s “employee handbook” style which reads too real.

Connor O'Brien: For release hygiene: add `SECURITY.md` + “Reporting vulnerabilities” section, and ensure the template repos ship with `.gitignore` that includes `.env`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, `.otel-collector.yaml` if it contains creds.

Marcus Lin: Specific callouts I saw in code review:
- Python: retry wrapper logs `repr(e)` which includes full URL + query params. If customers put tokens in query params (they shouldn’t, but they do), that gets logged.
- TS: fetch wrapper logs `requestInit` on failure (includes headers).
- Docker compose: example sets `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Bearer ..."` in a commented snippet. That’s risky even commented; people copy/paste.

Monica Patel: Good list. Let’s convert to action items.

Monica Patel: Proposed owners:
(1) Secrets in logs: Dana (TS), Sabrina (Python) w/ review by Hanae + Marcus
(2) Sample data: Wei
(3) Dependency pinning + scanning: Sahana
(4) OTel redaction processors + docs: Allison
(5) SECURITY.md + gitignore hardening: Connor
Everyone ok? ETAs by EOW if possible.

Dana Lo: 👍 I can land TS logging changes tomorrow. Will:
- replace raw `console.error(err)`
- ensure we never log headers/body
- add unit test for redaction (golden snapshot)

Hanae Suzuki: Can I add a small “secrets handling” doc snippet too? People keep using `.env` in production.

Monica Patel: Yes please. Add to the common README troubleshooting section.

Sabrina James: I’ll take Python logging sweep. I’ll grep for `exception(` / `extra={` / `headers` usage and ensure we use a `redact()` helper everywhere.
Also adding a test that asserts logs don’t contain `REDWOOD_API_KEY` when env is set.

Sahana Iyer: For pinning:
- Python: prefer `uv.lock` (fast + deterministic). If we’re not ready for uv, fallback to `requirements.txt` pinned with hashes.
- TS: lockfile + `packageManager` field.
I’ll also add Renovate config to batch updates weekly and require CI green.

Marcus Lin: One more: make sure telemetry tagging (template-origin) is opt-in and doesn’t include user identifiers. I know PM wants adoption metrics, but don’t default to sending extra headers that can trip proxies.

Monica Patel: Yep. Separate but related—telemetry header will be opt-in + documented. We’ll keep it out of this security checklist unless you think it blocks.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s partly security because “unknown headers” sometimes lead people to workaround by dumping more logs. But agree it’s not a hard block as long as opt-in + redaction.

Allison Grant: For collector config, I’ll ship:
- `attributes` processor deleting sensitive attrs
- `transform` processor to drop large payload fields
- examples for local vs prod
Also: add note that users shouldn’t set `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS` in compose files checked into git.

Wei Chen: Sample data rewrite: I’ll replace corpus with:
- synthetic “Redwood Inference docs” snippets
- a few short markdown files in `/sample_docs/`
- add `SAMPLE_DATA.md` explaining it’s synthetic + safe
ETA tomorrow.

Marcus Lin: Great. Blocking criteria for security sign-off:
- no secrets in logs/traces by default
- sample data is clearly synthetic + not derived from customer data
- dependencies pinned + scanning in CI
- collector redaction enabled by default (can be relaxed only with explicit config)

Monica Patel: Cool. I’ll track this against the launch checklist. Please reply in-thread when PRs are up so Marcus can re-review.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll open a PR today for CI + lockfiles and tag @Marcus Lin for a quick pass.

Dana Lo: PR coming shortly. Also removing the commented `Authorization=Bearer ...` snippet from compose and replacing with `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Bearer ${OTEL_BEARER_TOKEN}"` + doc note (and `.env` gitignored).

Hanae Suzuki: Love that. Also consider setting a max log field length (truncate) to avoid accidentally logging tool outputs/HTML/etc.

Sabrina James: +1. I’ll add truncation to the JSON logger formatter (e.g., 2k chars per field) with an allowlist for small fields.

Monica Patel: Thanks all. Next checkpoint: tomorrow EOD I’ll post status + what’s still open.

- Python: prefer `uv.lock` (fast + deterministic). If we’re not ready for uv, fallback to `requirements.txt` pinned with hashes.
- TS: lockfile + `packageManager` field.
I’ll also add Renovate config to batch updates weekly and require CI green.

Marcus Lin: One more: make sure telemetry tagging (template-origin) is opt-in and doesn’t include user identifiers. I know PM wants adoption metrics, but don’t default to sending extra headers that can trip proxies.

Monica Patel: Yep. Separate but related—telemetry header will be opt-in + documented. We’ll keep it out of this security checklist unless you think it blocks.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s partly security because “unknown headers” sometimes lead people to workaround by dumping more logs. But agree it’s not a hard block as long as opt-in + redaction.

Allison Grant: For collector config, I’ll ship:
- `attributes` processor deleting sensitive attrs
- `transform` processor to drop large payload fields
- examples for local vs prod
Also: add note that users shouldn’t set `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS` in compose files checked into git.

Wei Chen: Sample data rewrite: I’ll replace corpus with:
- synthetic “Redwood Inference docs” snippets
- a few short markdown files in `/sample_docs/`
- add `SAMPLE_DATA.md` explaining it’s synthetic + safe
ETA tomorrow.

Marcus Lin: Great. Blocking criteria for security sign-off:
- no secrets in logs/traces by default
- sample data is clearly synthetic + not derived from customer data
- dependencies pinned + scanning in CI
- collector redaction enabled by default (can be relaxed only with explicit config)

Monica Patel: Cool. I’ll track this against the launch checklist. Please reply in-thread when PRs are up so Marcus can re-review.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll open a PR today for CI + lockfiles and tag @Marcus Lin for a quick pass.

Dana Lo: PR coming shortly. Also removing the commented `Authorization=Bearer ...` snippet from compose and replacing with `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Bearer ${OTEL_BEARER_TOKEN}"` + doc note (and `.env` gitignored).

Hanae Suzuki: Love that. Also consider setting a max log field length (truncate) to avoid accidentally logging tool outputs/HTML/etc.

Sabrina James: +1. I’ll add truncation to the JSON logger formatter (e.g., 2k chars per field) with an allowlist for small fields.

Monica Patel: Thanks all. Next checkpoint: tomorrow EOD I’ll post status + what’s still open.

source.content:
Monica Patel: We need to settle template telemetry tagging for KPIs (activation funnel + TTFS). Goal: identify traffic generated by quickstarts/templates without logging anything sensitive. Proposal was a header/tag like `X-Redwood-Template` + `X-Redwood-Template-Version`. Thoughts/concerns before we bake into Python + TS scaffolds?

Logan Wright: From analytics side: we need something we can reliably filter on in gateway + Console. Ideal is a low-cardinality attribute that is constant per template, e.g. `rag-agent` + `v0.3.1`. We do NOT want user project name, repo name, hostname, etc.

Nikhil Sharma: +1. For KPI we only need “came from template” + which template. Also needs to be consistent across languages. Don’t care if it’s header vs query param as long as it survives request path.

Dana Lo: In TS template, easiest is to inject a default header in the SDK client init. But… headers can be mutated by user middleware, and some folks might run behind proxies that block unknown headers.

Brian Murphy: Gateway side: we can whitelist a small set of headers and drop the rest. But we need to validate values (allowlist) otherwise people will stuff random strings and we’ll regret it (cardinality + potential data leakage).

Marcus Lin: Security/privacy angle: anything we ship must be safe-by-default. If we automatically send tags, it needs to be guaranteed non-sensitive + not user-supplied. Also ensure we don’t accidentally log headers anywhere (app logs, access logs). Even if header is benign, “template tag” can become a carrier for PII if user can override.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Templates should avoid “free-form” telemetry fields entirely. If we do headers, they should be hardcoded constants and not read from env vars. Env vars are too easy for users to set to things like customer name.

Allison Grant: Observability side: we already put lots of attributes into spans. Please don’t add `template.name` as a span attribute unless it’s strictly low-card and sanitized. Better: set it as a metric label only if it’s fixed values, or put it in logs as a fixed field (again: not user controlled). Also confirm redaction processors won’t strip it.

Wei Chen: Docs note: we should explain what gets sent and how to disable it. Folks will ask “are you tracking my prompts?”. We need a clear statement: only template identifier/version, no prompt/content.

Monica Patel: OK, so constraints:
- must be low-card, fixed set of values
- must not be user-provided
- must not include customer/project identifiers
- must be easy to disable

Logan Wright: Exactly. Also: event pipeline prefers something like `x-redwood-template: rag-agent` and `x-redwood-template-version: 0.3.1`. Then gateway translates to an internal tag in request context.

Brian Murphy: If we do this, I’d like to enforce:
1) header names are fixed
2) values must match regex + allowlist from gateway config
3) if invalid, drop header and maybe add a debug response header? (or just silently drop)

Dana Lo: Silently dropping is probably fine, but for templates we can also surface in “doctor” command: “template telemetry disabled/blocked”.

Monica Patel: Re: disable: should we make it opt-in instead of opt-out? I’m leaning opt-in given proxies + security concerns.

Nikhil Sharma: For KPI accuracy, opt-out is nicer… but we can live with opt-in if we make the “on” path one command / one env var. What’s the least risky choice?

Marcus Lin: Default should be OFF. That’s the cleanest privacy stance. Otherwise we’ll be having review cycles with Legal. If default ON, we must guarantee it can’t be repurposed for PII (i.e., cannot be overridden), and we should still provide a clear notice.

Hanae Suzuki: Agree with Marcus. Also, anything “automatic” tends to end up in bug reports (“why is your SDK adding headers?”). If we do opt-in, we can justify it as “enable to help us improve templates”.

Logan Wright: Analytics can handle opt-in. We’ll update funnel definition: adoption metrics based on template CLI usage + optional runtime tag.

Allison Grant: Please don’t tie runtime tagging to any trace attributes that include URL paths with user identifiers. Keep it isolated.

Monica Patel: What about using the existing `User-Agent`? Like `redwood-quickstart/rag-agent (python)`.

Brian Murphy: Please no. User-Agent is notoriously messy + gets rewritten. Also ends up in logs everywhere. Better to use a dedicated header and explicitly control whether we forward/log it.

Dana Lo: For TS, we can add a wrapper:
```ts
const client = new RedwoodClient({
  apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY,
  defaultHeaders: enableTemplateTelemetry ? {
    'x-redwood-template': 'rag-agent',
    'x-redwood-template-version': '0.3.1'
  } : {}
})
```
Where `enableTemplateTelemetry` is `process.env.REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY === '1'`.

Marcus Lin: I’m slightly nervous even about env var gating if values are hardcoded but ok. Just ensure env var is boolean and does not influence the content. i.e., do NOT read template name/version from env.

Monica Patel: Yup. Template name/version are generated at scaffold time and committed in code as constants.

Logan Wright: One more thing: version. We should decide if we tag “template version” or “release channel”. Version is useful for troubleshooting upgrades, but also increases cardinality a bit (still manageable).

Allison Grant: Cardinality: template version is fine if it’s a reasonable number of releases. Don’t include git SHA.

Nikhil Sharma: Version is helpful for correlating to support tickets (“people on 0.2.x hitting 429s”). I’d keep it.

Wei Chen: In docs, we’ll say: `x-redwood-template` and `x-redwood-template-version` are optional headers added when you enable telemetry. They do not include prompt/user data.

Hanae Suzuki: Logging: templates must redact request headers by default in app logs. Some people turn on debug logging and it dumps headers.

Monica Patel: We already added log redaction defaults in PR-102. We can add explicit rule: drop any `x-redwood-*` headers from logs (even though ours are harmless). Defense in depth.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also make sure README says: “don’t log request/response bodies in prod.”

Brian Murphy: Gateway: I’ll coordinate with Brian Murphy (me) / platform team to allowlist these two headers and block everything else under `x-redwood-template*` to avoid future creep.

Logan Wright: Please also confirm with Console/Analytics that we can filter on internal tag, not raw header.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Monica/Dana: implement opt-in boolean env var `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=1` that adds fixed headers.
- Logan: update KPI spec to include optional runtime tag + CLI init event.
- Brian: gateway allowlist + value validation.
- Marcus/Hanae: quick security sign-off checklist: headers not logged, values constant.
- Wei: docs copy for privacy + how to enable/disable.

Nikhil Sharma: Sounds good. If we get pushback later, we can still run metrics off CLI init and docs page visits, but runtime tag would be great.

Allison Grant: Please ping me when you pick final attribute names. I want to ensure we don’t collide with OTel semantic conventions and that we keep it consistent across templates.

Monica Patel: Will do. Tentative names:
- headers: `x-redwood-template`, `x-redwood-template-version`
- internal metric dimension: `redwood.template`
- internal trace attribute (ONLY if needed): `redwood.template` (same value)
But we’ll keep trace attr off for now unless Logan says it’s required.

Logan Wright: Metrics dimension + gateway tag is enough. Trace attr not required.

Marcus Lin: Good. Keep it minimal.

Monica Patel: Cool. I’ll write this up in the requirements doc + link from the template checklist.

Dana Lo: We need to lock down our approach for tool schema validation in the agent templates. Seeing a bunch of failures in early runs where the model “mostly” follows the schema but not enough for code to trust it. Can we align on: (1) what we validate, (2) how strict, (3) how we surface to user + logs + traces.

Monica Patel: +1. Let’s make it consistent across Py + TS. I’d like the template to *never* pass unvalidated tool args into a tool impl.

Dana Lo: Concrete examples I’ve seen (TS template):
- Model calls `lookup_docs` with args: `{ "query": ["foo"] }` (array instead of string)
- Calls `lookup_docs` with `{ "q": "foo" }` (wrong key)
- Calls `lookup_docs` with `{ "query": "foo", "top_k": "5" }` (string)
- Tool returns `{ results: [...] }` but our prompt expects `{ documents: [...] }`
- Tool returns huge blob (>200KB) and we end up sending it back into the model -> token explosion
- Tool returns invalid JSON because tool printed logs into stdout and we captured it

Ibrahim Khan: Another common one: model hallucinated tool name. It’ll call `searchDocs` or `doc_search` instead of the registered `lookup_docs`. We should treat that separately from schema errors.

Sabrina James: From smoke tests: we also get partial JSON, esp when streaming. Example: model emits `{"query":"foo"` then stream ends. Needs a clean retry path.

Wei Chen: Docs-wise, we need a “common errors” section that explains what the template does when schema validation fails (retry vs fail fast), and what users can tweak.

Brian Murphy: Gateway/SDK perspective: we should not assume providers always enforce `json_schema` correctly. Even with “structured output” modes, we can get type mismatches. Template should still validate locally.

Monica Patel: Proposal:
1) Validate tool *call* payload (tool name, args) before invoking tool.
2) Validate tool *result* payload before handing it back to the agent loop (if we have a declared result schema).
3) Enforce hard caps: max tool output bytes/chars, max list sizes.

Allison Grant: + please make sure we emit consistent signals:
- metric counter: `quickstart.agent.tool_validation_error_total` (dim: `tool_name`, `error_type`) — keep dims low-cardinality
- span: `agent.tool.validate` as child of `agent.step` with attrs like `tool.name`, `validation.success` (boolean), `validation.error_type`
- log field: `error_code` from taxonomy, and include `redwood_request_id` if available

Marcus Lin: Security angle: do *not* log raw tool args/results by default. Those can include PII (doc chunks, emails, etc.). Log only:
- tool name
- sizes (bytes)
- which required keys missing / which types wrong (but not values)
If user opts into debug logs locally, fine, but prod default = redacted.

Dana Lo: What strictness do we want? Zod has `strict()` which rejects unknown keys; Pydantic can do `extra='forbid'`. That helps prevent prompt injection via “extra fields” getting threaded into code.

Ibrahim Khan: I’d go strict by default. If the model sends extra keys, we can either:
- reject + ask model to retry with correct schema
or
- strip unknown keys then validate
Stripping is “friendlier” but can be risky if unknown keys get used accidentally later.

Monica Patel: I prefer reject+retry for agent templates. “Production-minded by default” = correctness > convenience.

Sabrina James: Agree, but we need to cap retries. Otherwise it loops forever when the model is stuck. Maybe:
- per tool call: 1 retry for schema errors
- per agent run: max 2 schema-retry attempts total
And then fail with a clear error message.

Dana Lo: Here’s a real failure dump (sanitized):
```txt
ToolCallValidationError: lookup_docs
- expected args.query: string
- received args.query: array
- path: ["query"]
```
When this happens, I’m thinking we respond to the model with a tool error message like:
“Tool invocation failed validation. Expected schema: {query: string, top_k?: number}. Your args had query=array.”

Wei Chen: Please keep the error message short and deterministic. If we echo too much, users will paste it into Slack and it’ll contain doc content. Also, we should have a troubleshooting tip: “If this happens often, tighten your tool descriptions or use smaller tool schemas.”

Brian Murphy: +1 on deterministic. Also consider a specific error code, not just free-form text.

Monica Patel: Let’s align taxonomy (draft):
- `TOOL_NOT_FOUND`
- `TOOL_ARGS_SCHEMA_INVALID`
- `TOOL_RESULT_SCHEMA_INVALID`
- `TOOL_OUTPUT_TOO_LARGE`
- `TOOL_EXECUTION_ERROR` (tool threw)
- `TOOL_TIMEOUT`

Allison Grant: Works. For metrics, keep `error_code` as the dimension value, not arbitrary text.

Dana Lo: Implementation detail (TS):
- Define `ToolDefinition` with `argsSchema` (zod) and optional `resultSchema`
- Wrapper does:
  1) check toolName in registry
  2) `argsSchema.strict().parse(args)`
  3) run tool with validated args
  4) enforce size cap on raw tool output
  5) if `resultSchema` present, validate
  6) return validated result
If validation fails -> throw `ToolValidationError` with `error_code`.

Monica Patel: Python mirror:
- Pydantic models for args/result
- `ConfigDict(extra='forbid')`
- `model_validate()` on args
- same size cap + list caps (e.g., max 20 docs)

Marcus Lin: Size caps: make sure it’s not just “chars” because unicode + JSON overhead. But “bytes of JSON-encoded string” is fine.

Sabrina James: Streaming/partial JSON: do we retry the *model* call or just treat it as invalid args?

Ibrahim Khan: For tool calls, if we’re in a “structured output / tool call” mode and parsing fails due to incomplete JSON, I’d classify as `TOOL_ARGS_SCHEMA_INVALID` and do one retry on the LLM step (not on the tool). But we need to differentiate parse error vs schema mismatch.

Dana Lo: So two subtypes under args invalid:
- `TOOL_ARGS_PARSE_ERROR` (invalid JSON)
- `TOOL_ARGS_SCHEMA_INVALID` (valid JSON, wrong shape)
(Still roll up to same metric maybe.)

Allison Grant: Please don’t explode metric cardinality with sub-sub types. One `error_code` is fine if we keep it to the list above. If you really want parse vs schema, do it as a span attribute only.

Monica Patel: Ok: keep the public code list small. Internally in logs/spans we can include `validation_failure_kind=parse|schema|unknown`.

Wei Chen: User-facing behavior summary for README:
- Template validates tool args/results strictly
- On validation failure: retries once with a “schema reminder” message
- If still failing: stops with a clear error and pointers to where to adjust tool schemas
- Tool outputs are truncated/blocked above a safe limit (configurable)

Brian Murphy: Edge case: tool returns an object but includes an error field. Don’t let models “treat it as success.” Better to have tool throw and return `TOOL_EXECUTION_ERROR` rather than returning `{error: ...}` and hoping the model interprets.

Dana Lo: +1. We should standardize tool implementations to raise exceptions for failure and let wrapper map them.

Sabrina James: For tests, I’ll add fixtures:
- missing required
- wrong type
- extra keys
- unknown tool name
- oversized output
- invalid JSON / partial
and assert:
- correct error_code
- retry happens once
- span+log include tool.name + redwood_request_id

Monica Patel: Sounds good. Dana can you post a short snippet we can re-use across templates (TS first), then we mirror in Python.

Dana Lo: Yep. Draft snippet for TS:
```ts
const args = tool.argsSchema.strict().safeParse(rawArgs);
if (!args.success) {
  throw new ToolValidationError({
    errorCode: "TOOL_ARGS_SCHEMA_INVALID",
    toolName,
    details: summarizeZodError(args.error),
  });
}
```
And `summarizeZodError` returns paths + expected types only.

Marcus Lin: Please make sure `details` is safe-by-default (no values). :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: When you add the span, name it consistently and add `tool.name` attribute. Also pls propagate request id into logs. We’ll map it in dashboards.

Monica Patel: Decision: strict validation (forbid unknown keys) by default; 1 retry on args validation failure; size cap on tool outputs; minimal error codes; safe logging.

Dana Lo: I’ll implement in TS template + open a PR, then we can port.

Sabrina James: I’ll wire the smoke tests once PR is up.

Wei Chen: I’ll update troubleshooting doc once we have final error strings.

source.text:
Monica Patel: quick q for @Allison Grant / @Logan Wright — we’re wiring OTel into the quickstart templates (RAG + agents) and want to make sure we don’t shoot ourselves w/ cardinality. what attrs/dimensions are “allowed” for spans + metrics? we have some ideas (model, template_version, tool_name, vector_store) but worried about exploding tags.

Allison Grant: +1 raising early. we’ve been burned before. general rule: spans can tolerate more context, metrics cannot. also: never put user input (prompt, query, doc IDs) into attrs.

Logan Wright: yep. metrics: keep label set small + bounded. if you can’t enumerate possible values in advance, it’s probably not safe for metrics.

Monica Patel: makes sense. current draft dims for metrics:
- `service.name` (template app)
- `deployment.mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `model` (string)
- `http.route`
- `tool.name`
- `retriever.backend` (faiss/local, etc)
- `template.version`

Is `model` and `tool.name` too risky?

Caleb Johnson: depends. `model` can be ok if it’s pinned to a small supported set and normalized (e.g. `rw/model_family` or `rw.model_id` from an allowlist). if users can type arbitrary model names, it will balloon.

Allison Grant: +100. for templates, we should strongly encourage model pinning and only emit `rw.model` from a bounded set (or map unknown -> `custom`).

Sergio Costa: `tool.name` in agent templates can also explode if users add 20 tools w/ unique names. maybe use `tool.type` (e.g. `http`, `db`, `vector_search`, `filesystem`) rather than name? or only allowlist built-in tools.

Dana Lo: in TS template we were planning to name tools after functions (`get_user_by_email`, etc). definitely unbounded.

Christine Wong: + re: `http.route` — make sure it’s the route template, not full URL w/ IDs (`/users/:id` vs `/users/123`). we should be careful in examples.

Monica Patel: good call. in FastAPI/Express we can use route pattern.

Wei Chen: doc side question: do we want to tell users “don’t add dynamic labels” explicitly in README? or just hide it.

Allison Grant: we should be explicit. “if it looks like a UUID, don’t put it in tags.” also: “prefer logs for high-card debugging.”

Logan Wright: for Redwood Console dashboards, we mainly need a few stable breakdowns:
- route
- status (success/error)
- error.type (bounded)
- deployment.mode
- template (name + version)
- model (bounded)
Everything else should go to traces/logs.

Monica Patel: can we align on a concrete list for v1? like “required attrs” + “allowed attrs”?

Caleb Johnson: suggestion: define 3 buckets:
1) REQUIRED (must emit)
2) RECOMMENDED (emit if available, bounded)
3) TRACE-ONLY (ok on spans but do not use as metric labels)

Allison Grant: +1. here’s a strawman.

Allison Grant: REQUIRED (spans + metrics):
- `service.name` / `service.version` (template app + git sha or template version)
- `deployment.environment` (dev/staging/prod)
- `rw.deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `http.method`
- `http.route`
- `http.response.status_code`
- `rw.request_id` (TRACE ONLY maybe? but at least in logs)

RECOMMENDED (bounded, OK for metrics):
- `rw.template_name` (e.g. `rag-agent-python`)
- `rw.template_version` (e.g. `0.4.2`)
- `rw.model` (allowlist or `custom`)
- `rw.stream` (true/false)
- `rw.cache` (hit/miss/na)
- `rw.error_type` (network/auth/quota/timeout/tool/retrieval/validation/unknown)

TRACE-ONLY (spans only, NOT metrics labels):
- `rw.tool_name` (string)
- `rw.vector_store_collection` (string)
- `rw.retrieved_doc_count` (numeric ok as span attr; as metric use histogram w/o labels)
- `rw.conversation_id` (NO for metrics, probably NO entirely unless hashed?)

Hard NO (neither):
- prompts, queries, doc IDs, raw URLs w/ query params, user IDs/emails, API keys (obv)

Thoughts?

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, having `rw.tool_name` would be *nice* but agree trace-only. we can still compute tool failure rate w/ `rw.tool_type` as a bounded set.

Dana Lo: we can add `tool.type` in our wrapper (http/db/vector). tool name stays in logs.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on request_id: keep it out of metrics labels. add it to logs + trace attributes only. if we make it a metric label we’ll crash cardinality.

Logan Wright: agree. `rw.request_id` should be:
- trace attribute on the root span
- log field on every log line
- NOT a metric label

Christine Wong: also watch for `exception.message` and `error.message` attrs. OTel SDKs will happily attach giant strings. for templates, we should redact/limit length. helpful for debug but can be PII.

Allison Grant: yes. we have a redaction processor in the collector examples, but we should also sanitize in-app for logs.

Monica Patel: question on tokens metrics. we want `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, latency, retry_count. any concerns?

Caleb Johnson: tokens are fine as numeric metrics (counter/histogram). don’t label by `prompt_template` or `customer_id`. keep labels same as above.

Sergio Costa: also, `model` label + token counters is super useful. but again: only if bounded.

Monica Patel: ok. we can enforce `RW_MODEL` to be one of a documented list and map unknown -> `custom`.

Wei Chen: we should reflect that in docs: “if you change the model to a custom ID, metrics will show model=custom.” prevents surprise.

Dana Lo: for `http.client.*` attrs: do we use standard semantic conventions? (we’re instrumenting Redwood SDK calls)

Allison Grant: use OTel semantic conv where possible (`http.request.method`, `url.scheme`, etc) but please avoid `url.full` if it includes query params. prefer `server.address` + `server.port` + `http.route`.

Logan Wright: also: `net.peer.name`/`server.address` is typically low-card (api.redwood.ai) but in private deployments it could be internal hostnames. still bounded-ish, but I’d rather not label metrics by it.

Monica Patel: got it. keep endpoint/region as trace-only.

Christine Wong: re: region — might be ok as bounded dimension if we have like 5 regions. but if private/on-prem can be arbitrary. default to trace-only.

Allison Grant: yep: `rw.region` trace-only, unless it’s from a known Redwood set.

Monica Patel: action items:
1) DevEx will update template instrumentation to match buckets above
2) add `tool.type` (bounded) + move `tool.name` to logs/trace only
3) normalize `rw.model` to allowlist|custom
4) ensure `http.route` uses patterns
5) add docs note on cardinality + “NO PII in attrs”

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: i’ll also send a short snippet for an allowlist helper + examples.

Sergio Costa: i can adjust the dashboard pack assumptions (no tool_name breakdown).

Allison Grant: please paste the final attr list into the observability standard doc once updated. also if anyone adds new labels later, run it by us.

Monica Patel: will do. thanks all.

Allison Grant: REQUIRED (spans + metrics):

- `rw.request_id` (TRACE ONLY maybe? but at least in logs)

RECOMMENDED (bounded, OK for metrics):
- `rw.template_name` (e.g. `rag-agent-python`)
- `rw.template_version` (e.g. `0.4.2`)
- `rw.model` (allowlist or `custom`)
- `rw.stream` (true/false)
- `rw.cache` (hit/miss/na)
- `rw.error_type` (network/auth/quota/timeout/tool/retrieval/validation/unknown)

TRACE-ONLY (spans only, NOT metrics labels):
- `rw.tool_name` (string)
- `rw.vector_store_collection` (string)
- `rw.retrieved_doc_count` (numeric ok as span attr; as metric use histogram w/o labels)
- `rw.conversation_id` (NO for metrics, probably NO entirely unless hashed?)

Hard NO (neither):
- prompts, queries, doc IDs, raw URLs w/ query params, user IDs/emails, API keys (obv)

Thoughts?

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, having `rw.tool_name` would be *nice* but agree trace-only. we can still compute tool failure rate w/ `rw.tool_type` as a bounded set.

Dana Lo: we can add `tool.type` in our wrapper (http/db/vector). tool name stays in logs.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on request_id: keep it out of metrics labels. add it to logs + trace attributes only. if we make it a metric label we’ll crash cardinality.

Logan Wright: agree. `rw.request_id` should be:
- trace attribute on the root span
- log field on every log line
- NOT a metric label

Christine Wong: also watch for `exception.message` and `error.message` attrs. OTel SDKs will happily attach giant strings. for templates, we should redact/limit length. helpful for debug but can be PII.

Allison Grant: yes. we have a redaction processor in the collector examples, but we should also sanitize in-app for logs.

Monica Patel: question on tokens metrics. we want `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, latency, retry_count. any concerns?

Caleb Johnson: tokens are fine as numeric metrics (counter/histogram). don’t label by `prompt_template` or `customer_id`. keep labels same as above.

Sergio Costa: also, `model` label + token counters is super useful. but again: only if bounded.

Monica Patel: ok. we can enforce `RW_MODEL` to be one of a documented list and map unknown -> `custom`.

Wei Chen: we should reflect that in docs: “if you change the model to a custom ID, metrics will show model=custom.” prevents surprise.

Dana Lo: for `http.client.*` attrs: do we use standard semantic conventions? (we’re instrumenting Redwood SDK calls)

Allison Grant: use OTel semantic conv where possible (`http.request.method`, `url.scheme`, etc) but please avoid `url.full` if it includes query params. prefer `server.address` + `server.port` + `http.route`.

Logan Wright: also: `net.peer.name`/`server.address` is typically low-card (api.redwood.ai) but in private deployments it could be internal hostnames. still bounded-ish, but I’d rather not label metrics by it.

Monica Patel: got it. keep endpoint/region as trace-only.

Christine Wong: re: region — might be ok as bounded dimension if we have like 5 regions. but if private/on-prem can be arbitrary. default to trace-only.

Allison Grant: yep: `rw.region` trace-only, unless it’s from a known Redwood set.

Monica Patel: action items:
1) DevEx will update template instrumentation to match buckets above
2) add `tool.type` (bounded) + move `tool.name` to logs/trace only
3) normalize `rw.model` to allowlist|custom
4) ensure `http.route` uses patterns
5) add docs note on cardinality + “NO PII in attrs”

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: i’ll also send a short snippet for an allowlist helper + examples.

Sergio Costa: i can adjust the dashboard pack assumptions (no tool_name breakdown).

Allison Grant: please paste the final attr list into the observability standard doc once updated. also if anyone adds new labels later, run it by us.

Monica Patel: will do. thanks all.
text:
Monica Patel: quick q for @Allison Grant / @Logan Wright — we’re wiring OTel into the quickstart templates (RAG + agents) and want to make sure we don’t shoot ourselves w/ cardinality. what attrs/dimensions are “allowed” for spans + metrics? we have some ideas (model, template_version, tool_name, vector_store) but worried about exploding tags.

Allison Grant: +1 raising early. we’ve been burned before. general rule: spans can tolerate more context, metrics cannot. also: never put user input (prompt, query, doc IDs) into attrs.

Logan Wright: yep. metrics: keep label set small + bounded. if you can’t enumerate possible values in advance, it’s probably not safe for metrics.

Monica Patel: makes sense. current draft dims for metrics:
- `service.name` (template app)
- `deployment.mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `model` (string)
- `http.route`
- `tool.name`
- `retriever.backend` (faiss/local, etc)
- `template.version`

Is `model` and `tool.name` too risky?

Caleb Johnson: depends. `model` can be ok if it’s pinned to a small supported set and normalized (e.g. `rw/model_family` or `rw.model_id` from an allowlist). if users can type arbitrary model names, it will balloon.

Allison Grant: +100. for templates, we should strongly encourage model pinning and only emit `rw.model` from a bounded set (or map unknown -> `custom`).

Sergio Costa: `tool.name` in agent templates can also explode if users add 20 tools w/ unique names. maybe use `tool.type` (e.g. `http`, `db`, `vector_search`, `filesystem`) rather than name? or only allowlist built-in tools.

Dana Lo: in TS template we were planning to name tools after functions (`get_user_by_email`, etc). definitely unbounded.

Christine Wong: + re: `http.route` — make sure it’s the route template, not full URL w/ IDs (`/users/:id` vs `/users/123`). we should be careful in examples.

Monica Patel: good call. in FastAPI/Express we can use route pattern.

Wei Chen: doc side question: do we want to tell users “don’t add dynamic labels” explicitly in README? or just hide it.

Allison Grant: we should be explicit. “if it looks like a UUID, don’t put it in tags.” also: “prefer logs for high-card debugging.”

Logan Wright: for Redwood Console dashboards, we mainly need a few stable breakdowns:
- route
- status (success/error)
- error.type (bounded)
- deployment.mode
- template (name + version)
- model (bounded)
Everything else should go to traces/logs.

1) REQUIRED (must emit)
2) RECOMMENDED (emit if available, bounded)
3) TRACE-ONLY (ok on spans but do not use as metric labels)

Allison Grant: +1. here’s a strawman.

Allison Grant: REQUIRED (spans + metrics):
- `service.name` / `service.version` (template app + git sha or template version)

- `http.method`
- `http.route`
- `http.response.status_code`
- `rw.request_id` (TRACE ONLY maybe? but at least in logs)

RECOMMENDED (bounded, OK for metrics):
- `rw.template_name` (e.g. `rag-agent-python`)
- `rw.template_version` (e.g. `0.4.2`)
- `rw.model` (allowlist or `custom`)
- `rw.stream` (true/false)
- `rw.cache` (hit/miss/na)
- `rw.error_type` (network/auth/quota/timeout/tool/retrieval/validation/unknown)

TRACE-ONLY (spans only, NOT metrics labels):
- `rw.tool_name` (string)
- `rw.vector_store_collection` (string)
- `rw.retrieved_doc_count` (numeric ok as span attr; as metric use histogram w/o labels)
- `rw.conversation_id` (NO for metrics, probably NO entirely unless hashed?)

Hard NO (neither):
- prompts, queries, doc IDs, raw URLs w/ query params, user IDs/emails, API keys (obv)

Thoughts?

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, having `rw.tool_name` would be *nice* but agree trace-only. we can still compute tool failure rate w/ `rw.tool_type` as a bounded set.

Dana Lo: we can add `tool.type` in our wrapper (http/db/vector). tool name stays in logs.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on request_id: keep it out of metrics labels. add it to logs + trace attributes only. if we make it a metric label we’ll crash cardinality.

Logan Wright: agree. `rw.request_id` should be:
- trace attribute on the root span
- log field on every log line
- NOT a metric label

Christine Wong: also watch for `exception.message` and `error.message` attrs. OTel SDKs will happily attach giant strings. for templates, we should redact/limit length. helpful for debug but can be PII.

Allison Grant: yes. we have a redaction processor in the collector examples, but we should also sanitize in-app for logs.

Monica Patel: question on tokens metrics. we want `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, latency, retry_count. any concerns?

Caleb Johnson: tokens are fine as numeric metrics (counter/histogram). don’t label by `prompt_template` or `customer_id`. keep labels same as above.

Sergio Costa: also, `model` label + token counters is super useful. but again: only if bounded.

Wei Chen: we should reflect that in docs: “if you change the model to a custom ID, metrics will show model=custom.” prevents surprise.

Allison Grant: use OTel semantic conv where possible (`http.request.method`, `url.scheme`, etc) but please avoid `url.full` if it includes query params. prefer `server.address` + `server.port` + `http.route`.

Logan Wright: also: `net.peer.name`/`server.address` is typically low-card (api.redwood.ai) but in private deployments it could be internal hostnames. still bounded-ish, but I’d rather not label metrics by it.

source.messages:
Dev Patel: triage: we now have 3 auth-related customer reports across SDKs (SUP-4821 python, SUP-4884 ts edge streaming, SUP-4902 go newline). want to track counts per SDK for the QBR doc.
Ethan Park: python fix is merged as redwood-sdk-python pr-891 and released v0.9.4.
Dev Patel: TS fix in flight, will be redwood-sdk-typescript pr-512 (Authorization header dropped in edge streaming).
Elliot Price: Go fix is redwood-sdk-go pr-233; newline trim + clearer error.
Ava Chen: I just got another python one: customer passed api_key='Bearer <key>' and we double-prefixed -> 401. that's SUP-4933.
Logan Wright: we should add a shared doc: 'raw key only' + 'edge runtime header behavior'.
Ethan Park: +1. also, for reporting: question is customer-reported auth bugs, so SUP-4933 should count for python too even if it's config footgun.
Dev Patel: agreed. Ill update the internal sheet.

source.text:
Monica Patel: dropping the SDK “production defaults” proposal here for review. TL;DR: we’ve got too many first-prod launch issues that are basically missing timeouts/retries + weird streaming disconnect behavior. Proposal is to ship sane presets across Python/TS/Go and update quickstarts/snippets to use them. Question: do we make these actual *defaults* or do we keep current behavior and expose an opt-in preset? Biggest worry is breaking folks who rely on “infinite” requests or who are doing their own retries.

Monica Patel: draft defaults (if we went that route):
- non-streaming: connect timeout 5s, request timeout 60s, retries: 2 (only on 408/409/429/5xx), backoff w/ jitter, respect `Retry-After`
- streaming: connect timeout 5s, no overall request timeout (or very high), but heartbeat / read timeout 30s, and reconnect helper (no auto-retry by default)
- also: emit `User-Agent: redwood-sdk/<ver> preset=prod` tag so Telemetry can attribute funnel

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on presets, -1 on silently changing defaults. Any “retry by default” in SDK land becomes a support magnet when customers see duplicate side effects (even if most endpoints are idempotent-ish).

Ibrahim Khan: for chat/generate, server side *should* be idempotent given same input, but customers sometimes include things like tool calls that trigger external actions. we can’t assume “safe to retry” at the client.

Brian Murphy: agree. also OpenAI-compat users will assume OpenAI semantics (no automatic retries unless they add them). If we change defaults it’ll be a “why did you retry this?” surprise.

Dana Lo: on TS specifically: implementing a hard request timeout by default is risky because `fetch` + streaming is weird. If we set a global `AbortController` deadline, we’ll kill long-lived streams and people will file “streaming randomly stops at 60s.”

Dana Lo: I’d rather provide helpers:
- `client = new Redwood({ retry: RedwoodRetry.production(), timeout: RedwoodTimeouts.production() })`
- and a separate `stream()` helper that supports reconnect logic with an explicit opt-in.

Wei Chen: from docs side, I’m fine with “recommended defaults” / presets and updating quickstart snippets to include them. That still moves TTFS/TTFP without us taking on backward compatibility risk.

Wei Chen: also: we should be super explicit in docs that streaming needs different handling. We keep seeing folks run streaming from local scripts with no keepalive and then they think the model is broken.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go thoughts: we should not hide deadlines. idiomatic Go is context-based timeouts. If we change defaults we’ll fight Go expectations.

Mateusz Zielinski: proposal: in go sdk, keep the core client “dumb”, but ship:
- `redwood.ProductionClient()` that sets transport timeouts + retry policy
- `StreamWithReconnect(ctx, req, opts)` helper

Mateusz Zielinski: also note: if we retry on 429 but the caller is already retrying, it doubles. docs should include “disable built-in retries if you already have a retry layer.”

Logan Wright: +1 to the `User-Agent` tagging, but we need to make sure it doesn’t explode cardinality. `preset=prod` is fine. please no dynamic values (like timeout ms) in UA.

Nadia Rahman: re: respect `Retry-After`: we need to be careful. sometimes gateway sends `Retry-After: 0` / missing header depending on the path. SDK should have sane fallback backoff.

Nadia Rahman: also we should align with what gateway actually returns for 429 today (some endpoints use `x-ratelimit-reset-ms`). If we can’t guarantee headers, SDK behavior should be documented (and not “magical”).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m nervous about defaults that can amplify traffic during an incident. Even “2 retries” can turn into a thundering herd if there’s a partial outage.

Sean Gallagher: if we do built-in retries, please cap by total retry time and add jitter. And ideally have a global kill switch via env var (or config) so support can ask customers to disable quickly.

Sabrina James: quickstarts/snippets angle: if presets exist but aren’t used by default, we *must* update the snippet generator to use `preset=production` in all languages. otherwise we’ll keep teaching unsafe usage.

Sabrina James: also, should the preset enable structured logging hooks? at least request id + status + latency (but obviously avoid prompt content).

Elliot Price: docs IA: if we don’t change defaults, we should avoid wording like “the SDK retries by default.” It should be “enable retries with the production preset” + show how to tune.

Monica Patel: ok sounds like consensus is “opt-in preset”, not changing defaults globally. what about making it the default only for *new API keys / new projects* via Console? (ex: copy/paste snippet includes it)

Brian Murphy: that’s the right place to enforce. Console snippet + docs + checklist step == user chooses it.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. We can also add a warning in Console if they haven’t configured any timeout/retry and they’re about to set a webhook/prod traffic. (not MVP but later)

Dana Lo: TS detail: for streaming, I’m strongly against auto-reconnect “by default.” But I’m good with `client.chat.streamWithReconnect()` that’s clearly labeled and requires an idempotency key or at least warns.

Mateusz Zielinski: same for Go. helpers, not magic.

Logan Wright: for onboarding funnel, we can also treat “production preset enabled” as a checklist step completion event. Emitted server-side via UA + request headers.

Nadia Rahman: +1, but make sure we don’t treat it as “completed” if they just installed SDK. should be on first successful 2xx with UA tag.

Monica Patel: action items I’m hearing:
1) Ship `production` presets for timeouts/retries in Python/TS/Go (opt-in)
2) Update snippet generator + quickstarts to use preset by default
3) Add `User-Agent` tag `preset=production` (static) for attribution
4) Define retry policy precisely (only 408/409/429/5xx; never on 4xx other than 408/409/429; respect headers when present; cap total retry time; jitter)
5) Streaming: provide reconnect helper but keep off by default; add docs on heartbeat/read timeout expectations

Monica Patel: if no objections, I’ll update the doc + open the Linear tickets under ENG-5820 for implementation details. please drop any edge cases we’re missing.

Sean Gallagher: one more: make sure retries don’t retry *after* a request has started streaming tokens. that can create weird partial responses.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. once bytes are received, don’t retry. also should surface “retry_count” in logs so customers can see what happened.

Dana Lo: ack. I’ll take a stab at TS API shape and post in-thread later today.

Sabrina James: I’ll sync with Wei on snippet updates. Once preset names are final, we can update quickstarts + templates.

Monica Patel: perfect, thanks all. :thumbsup:

Dana Lo: I’d rather provide helpers:
- `client = new Redwood({ retry: RedwoodRetry.production(), timeout: RedwoodTimeouts.production() })`
- and a separate `stream()` helper that supports reconnect logic with an explicit opt-in.

Wei Chen: from docs side, I’m fine with “recommended defaults” / presets and updating quickstart snippets to include them. That still moves TTFS/TTFP without us taking on backward compatibility risk.

Wei Chen: also: we should be super explicit in docs that streaming needs different handling. We keep seeing folks run streaming from local scripts with no keepalive and then they think the model is broken.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go thoughts: we should not hide deadlines. idiomatic Go is context-based timeouts. If we change defaults we’ll fight Go expectations.

Mateusz Zielinski: proposal: in go sdk, keep the core client “dumb”, but ship:
- `redwood.ProductionClient()` that sets transport timeouts + retry policy
- `StreamWithReconnect(ctx, req, opts)` helper

Mateusz Zielinski: also note: if we retry on 429 but the caller is already retrying, it doubles. docs should include “disable built-in retries if you already have a retry layer.”

Logan Wright: +1 to the `User-Agent` tagging, but we need to make sure it doesn’t explode cardinality. `preset=prod` is fine. please no dynamic values (like timeout ms) in UA.

Nadia Rahman: re: respect `Retry-After`: we need to be careful. sometimes gateway sends `Retry-After: 0` / missing header depending on the path. SDK should have sane fallback backoff.

Nadia Rahman: also we should align with what gateway actually returns for 429 today (some endpoints use `x-ratelimit-reset-ms`). If we can’t guarantee headers, SDK behavior should be documented (and not “magical”).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m nervous about defaults that can amplify traffic during an incident. Even “2 retries” can turn into a thundering herd if there’s a partial outage.

Sean Gallagher: if we do built-in retries, please cap by total retry time and add jitter. And ideally have a global kill switch via env var (or config) so support can ask customers to disable quickly.

Sabrina James: quickstarts/snippets angle: if presets exist but aren’t used by default, we *must* update the snippet generator to use `preset=production` in all languages. otherwise we’ll keep teaching unsafe usage.

Sabrina James: also, should the preset enable structured logging hooks? at least request id + status + latency (but obviously avoid prompt content).

Elliot Price: docs IA: if we don’t change defaults, we should avoid wording like “the SDK retries by default.” It should be “enable retries with the production preset” + show how to tune.

Monica Patel: ok sounds like consensus is “opt-in preset”, not changing defaults globally. what about making it the default only for *new API keys / new projects* via Console? (ex: copy/paste snippet includes it)

Brian Murphy: that’s the right place to enforce. Console snippet + docs + checklist step == user chooses it.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. We can also add a warning in Console if they haven’t configured any timeout/retry and they’re about to set a webhook/prod traffic. (not MVP but later)

Dana Lo: TS detail: for streaming, I’m strongly against auto-reconnect “by default.” But I’m good with `client.chat.streamWithReconnect()` that’s clearly labeled and requires an idempotency key or at least warns.

Mateusz Zielinski: same for Go. helpers, not magic.

Logan Wright: for onboarding funnel, we can also treat “production preset enabled” as a checklist step completion event. Emitted server-side via UA + request headers.

Nadia Rahman: +1, but make sure we don’t treat it as “completed” if they just installed SDK. should be on first successful 2xx with UA tag.

Monica Patel: action items I’m hearing:
1) Ship `production` presets for timeouts/retries in Python/TS/Go (opt-in)
2) Update snippet generator + quickstarts to use preset by default
3) Add `User-Agent` tag `preset=production` (static) for attribution
4) Define retry policy precisely (only 408/409/429/5xx; never on 4xx other than 408/409/429; respect headers when present; cap total retry time; jitter)
5) Streaming: provide reconnect helper but keep off by default; add docs on heartbeat/read timeout expectations

Monica Patel: if no objections, I’ll update the doc + open the Linear tickets under ENG-5820 for implementation details. please drop any edge cases we’re missing.

Sean Gallagher: one more: make sure retries don’t retry *after* a request has started streaming tokens. that can create weird partial responses.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. once bytes are received, don’t retry. also should surface “retry_count” in logs so customers can see what happened.

Dana Lo: ack. I’ll take a stab at TS API shape and post in-thread later today.

Sabrina James: I’ll sync with Wei on snippet updates. Once preset names are final, we can update quickstarts + templates.

Monica Patel: perfect, thanks all. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Alex Martinez: Kicking off the guided onboarding + “first production launch” checklist work. Goal is to reduce TTFS + TTFP and kill the usual 401/403 + streaming confusion + 429 surprise tickets.

Alex Martinez: Can folks drop in here: (1) who’s DRI for what, and (2) quick brainstorm of checklist steps + what *counts* as “first success”.

Talia Benson: I’ll run program mgmt for this (timeline, weekly sync, dependency tracking). I’ll send a calendar invite for a 30min kickoff.

Nikhil Sharma: PM Hosted API side: I’m in for product decisions + definitions on TTFS/TTFP (and exclusions). Also want to align this with PM-751 so the metric is stable.

Marissa Cole: Console PM: I’ll own where this lives in Console + how deep links work. Dylan/Chloe will own implementation pieces.

Dylan Brooks: Console Eng lead here. We can scope UI to a checklist module + step detail drawer + deep links. Need clarity on step IDs + state model soon so we don’t paint ourselves into a corner.

Chloe Martin: Console backend: I’ll coordinate with Ethan/Logan on progress service API shape + auth. Also: we should be explicit about how steps transition (no “regressions” when events arrive out of order).

Monica Patel: SDK lead. We’ll take “prod-safe defaults” guidance + snippet updates across Python/TS/Go + OpenAI compat. Ibrahim can drive streaming/retry defaults proposal.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Biggest new-user footguns I see: default timeout too low, no retries on 429/5xx, and streaming disconnects treated as success. We should define acceptance criteria per language.

Logan Wright: Telemetry/platform: I’ll own event schema + emission points proposal (likely server-side gateway/control plane). Chris/Mei can help with funnel + TTFS dashboard once definition is locked.

Chris Osei: I’ll partner with Logan on KPI definitions + data QA checks (freshness, cardinality). But we need a crisp TTFS definition to avoid moving targets.

Wei Chen: Docs/quickstarts: I can own the “first successful call” quickstart updates + make sure the checklist doc links map 1:1. Need the step list + copy direction from Jean-Paul/Design.

Elliot Price: Docs IA: I’ll own redirect/IA updates so users don’t bounce between old quickstarts + new checklist. Also want to ensure “production launch” steps have canonical docs pages (quotas/budgets, model pinning, fallback, observability hooks).

Jean-Paul Laurent: Content: I’ll draft step titles + acceptance criteria + concise copy (Console + docs). Please share any “must include” warnings (rate limits, usage dashboard lag, etc.).

Alex Martinez: Awesome. Let’s talk “first success” definition since it drives the first 2-3 checklist steps + the KPI.

Alex Martinez: Proposed: TTFS = time from signup -> first *successful* API response for a generation/embeddings endpoint (2xx). Not counting 401/403/429. Question: does streaming count only when stream is established? or do we require full completion?

Logan Wright: For clean instrumentation, I’d strongly prefer server-side: first request that returns HTTP 200 and includes `x-redwood-request-id` (or equivalent) + passes auth + is not a dry-run. We can also emit a separate event for “stream established” (first SSE chunk) vs “stream completed”.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 for separating. If we gate TTFS on stream *completion* we’ll make metrics noisy (clients disconnect mid-stream). But “stream established” is a meaningful success moment for onboarding.

Monica Patel: Agree. For non-streaming, “success” could be: response returned and parsed (server can’t tell parse success). But at least server-side 2xx + non-empty `usage.total_tokens` is a decent proxy.

Nikhil Sharma: What endpoints count? Chat + completions + embeddings? I’d include embeddings because some customers start there, but we should avoid “/models list” counting as success.

Chris Osei: If we include embeddings, make sure we don’t count “0-vector” or test payload errors. Maybe acceptance criteria: 2xx AND usage tokens > 0 for gen/chat, and embeddings count > 0 for embeddings.

Wei Chen: From docs POV, quickstart uses chat streaming by default. If TTFS excludes streaming establishment it’ll look “broken” vs what users do.

Marissa Cole: In Console checklist, we can have Step 1: “Make your first API call” (counts on first 2xx gen/chat/embeddings). Step 2: “Try streaming” (counts on stream established). Keeps it intuitive.

Dylan Brooks: Works. We’ll need stable step IDs: `first_successful_request`, `first_streaming_response`, etc. And deep links from step details to quickstart + “create API key” page.

Chloe Martin: Also need edge cases: user rotates keys, multiple workspaces, or runs from CI. Progress should be tenant/org scoped, not per user.

Logan Wright: Yup. Recommend scope = tenant. Event payload should include: tenant_id, user_id (optional), api_key_id (hashed?), sdk_lang, sdk_version, endpoint, response_code, model (bounded), is_streaming.

Jean-Paul Laurent: Quick brainstorm of step list (MVP):
1) Create API key
2) Set env var / connect SDK
3) Make first successful request (TTFS)
4) Try streaming
5) Configure timeouts + retries
6) View usage + costs dashboard
7) Set quota/budget alert
8) Pin model version
9) Configure fallback policy
10) Add basic observability hooks (request id + tracing)

Elliot Price: +1 list. We should be careful with order: “usage dashboard” has lag sometimes; copy should set expectation (“may take up to X min”). Also doc link should go to a single canonical page.

Monica Patel: For step 5, we can ship recommended defaults + a “preset” in SDKs, but checklist acceptance criteria should not require user to change code. Maybe: user has seen recommended config + acknowledged? (or we infer from UA tags / SDK options?)

Logan Wright: We can emit an event when SDK uses “prod preset” (client hint via user-agent tag) but avoid making that required for TTFS. Keep it as separate step completion.

Ibrahim Khan: I can add UA tags + a `retryPreset: "standard"` kind of setting in TS/Python. Go might need a helper.

Alex Martinez: Great. Action items:
- Logan + Chris: propose TTFS/TTFP definition + event names by EOW (tie to PM-751).
- Jean-Paul: draft step copy + acceptance criteria v0 (Console-friendly).
- Wei + Elliot: map steps -> docs/quickstarts; call out gaps.
- Monica/Ibrahim: outline SDK “prod defaults” approach + what we can detect (UA tags, config flags).

Talia Benson: I’ll capture the above + start a shared doc + weekly 30 min sync. Also can we list DRIs explicitly?

Alex Martinez: DRIs:
- Program: Talia
- Product: Nikhil (Hosted), Marissa (Console)
- Console Eng: Dylan/Chloe
- Telemetry: Logan (+ Chris for reporting)
- SDKs: Monica (+ Ibrahim)
- Docs: Elliot (+ Wei)
- Content: Jean-Paul

Marissa Cole: One more q: “signup” timestamp for TTFS start. Is it first user creation? first org created? first API key created?

Chris Osei: We should define start as `tenant_created_at` (org/workspace creation) to be consistent, but also track “time to API key created” as a separate step/funnel.

Logan Wright: Agree. `tenant_created` -> `api_key_created` -> `first_successful_request` funnel.

Nikhil Sharma: Please also exclude internal/dogfood tenants from the main KPI dashboards or tag them. Otherwise rollout will look artificially good/bad.

Dylan Brooks: +1. Feature flag cohorts will also matter for interpretation.

Alex Martinez: Cool. Keep this thread for loose brainstorms; Talia will send the kickoff + doc link shortly. :thumbsup:
